simple: The largest trial in the review (with 396 women) has not yet reported full results.

label: ignore
complex: The evidence suggests that for a woman with a 53% chance of live birth using standard IVF, the chance using natural cycle IVF would range from 34% to 53%.
simple: The evidence suggested that for a woman with a 53% chance of live birth using standard IVF, the chance using natural cycle IVF ranges from 34% to 53%.

label: rephrase
complex: There was no evidence of a statistically significant difference between natural cycle and standard IVF in rates of OHSS (OR 0.19, 95% CI 0.01 to 4.06, one study, 60 women, very low quality evidence), clinical pregnancy (OR 0.52 95% CI 0.17 to 1.61, 4 studies, 351 women, I2=63%, low quality evidence), ongoing pregnancy (OR 0.72, 95% CI 0.50 to 1.05, three studies, 485 women, I2=0%, moderate quality evidence), multiple pregnancy (OR 0.76, 95% CI 0.25 to 2.31, 2 studies, 527 women, I2=0%, very low quality evidence), gestational abnormalities (OR 0.44 95% CI 0.03 to 5.93, 1 study, 18 women, very low quality evidence) or cycle cancellations (OR 8.98, 95% CI 0.20 to 393.66, 2 studies, 159 women, I2=83%, very low quality evidence).
simple: No significant difference was found in rates of clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, multiple pregnancy, incidence of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome, gestational abnormalities or cancellations of treatment.

label: split
complex: Findings on treatment costs were inconsistent and more data are awaited.
simple: However findings were imprecise for all outcomes and further evidence from larger studies is awaited. Findings on cost-effectiveness were inconsistent.

label: rephrase
complex: The evidence was limited by imprecision.
simple: Quality ratings for the evidence ranged from very low to moderate, the main limitation being imprecision due to insufficient data.

label: rephrase
complex: Findings for pregnancy rate and for cycle cancellation were sensitive to the choice of statistical model: for these outcomes, use of a fixed effect model suggested a benefit for the standard IVF group.
simple: When the review authors checked the effect of using an alternative method of analysis the findings suggested higher rates of clinical pregnancy with standard IVF than with natural cycle IVF.

label: rephrase
complex: No studies reported the effect of meglitinides on mortality or morbidity.
simple: No studies reported the effect of meglitinides on mortality or diabetes related complications.

label: rephrase
complex: In the eleven studies comparing meglitinides to placebo, both repaglinide and nateglinide resulted in a reductions in glycosylated haemoglobin (0.1% to 2.1% reduction in HbA1c for repaglinide; 0.2% to 0.6% for nateglinide).
simple: In the eleven studies comparing meglitinides to placebo, both repaglinide and nateglinide resulted in an improved blood sugar control.

label: rephrase
complex: Weight gain was generally greater in those treated with meglitinides compared with metformin (up to three kg in three months).
simple: Weight gain was generally greater in those treated with meglitinides compared with metformin (up to three kg in three months), another oral antidiabetic drug.

label: ignore
complex: Diarrhoea occurred less frequently and hypoglycaemia occurred more frequently but rarely severely enough as to require assistance.
simple: Here, diarrhoea occurred less frequently and hypoglycaemia occurred more frequently but rarely severely enough as to require assistance.

label: rephrase
complex: Meglitinides may offer an alternative oral hypoglycaemic agent of similar potency to metformin, and may be indicated where side effects of metformin are intolerable or where metformin is contraindicated.
simple: Meglitinides may offer an alternative oral hypoglycaemic agent of similar potency to metformin, and may be indicated where side effects of metformin are intolerable (in particular persistent diarrhoea) or where metformin is contraindicated.

label: split
complex: However, there is no evidence available to indicate what effect meglitinides will have on important long-term outcomes, particularly mortality.
simple: However, there is no evidence available yet to indicate what effect meglitinides will have on important long-term outcomes, in particular, on mortality. As yet, the experience with meglitinides in terms of side effects is limited.

label: rephrase
complex: All the trials were of high risk of bias.
simple: All the trials were at high risk of systematic errors (ie, there was potential to arrive at wrong conclusions because of the way the trial was conducted) and random errors (there was potential to arrive at the wrong conclusions because of the play of chance).

label: ignore
complex: Ten trials including 441 liver transplant recipients provided data for this review.
simple: Ten trials including 441 liver transplant recipients provided data for this review.

label: none
complex: There were no significant differences in the retransplantation at maximal follow-up (2 trials; 105 participants; 2/47 (adjusted proportion: 4.0%) in interferon group versus 2/58 (3.4%) in control group; RR 1.17; 95% CI 0.22 to 6.2); 90-day retransplantation (1 trial; 18 participants; 1/12 (8.3%) in HCV antibody group versus 0/6 (0%) in control group; RR 1.71; 95% CI 0.09 to 32.93); or 90-day retransplantation (1 trial; 12 participants; 1/6 (17.7%) in HCV antibody high-dose group versus 0/6 (0%) in HCV antibody low-dose group; RR 3.00; 95% CI 0.15 to 61.74).
simple: There were no significant differences in the proportion of patients who died or required retransplantation within 90 days or at maximal follow-up between the different treatment groups for any of the comparisons.

label: rephrase
complex: There were no significant differences in serious adverse events, graft rejection, worsening of fibrosis, or HCV recurrence between intervention and control groups in any of the comparisons that reported these outcomes.
simple: There were no significant differences in serious complications, graft rejection, microscopic features of liver damage, or evidence of chronic hepatitis C recurrence between the different treatment groups or no treatment in any of the comparisons that reported these outcomes.

label: ignore
complex: None of the trials reported quality of life, liver decompensation, intensive therapy unit stay, or hospital stay.
simple: None of the trials reported quality of life, liver failure, intensive therapy unit stay, or hospital stay.

label: rephrase
complex: Life-threatening adverse events were not reported in either group in any of the comparisons.
simple: Life-threatening adverse events were not reported in any of the comparisons.

label: ignore
complex: There is currently no evidence to recommend prophylactic antiviral treatment to prevent recurrence of HCV infection either in primary liver transplantation or retransplantation.
simple: There is currently no evidence to recommend preventive antiviral treatment to prevent recurrence of chronic HCV infection either in primary liver transplantation or retransplantation.

label: rephrase
complex: Further randomised clinical trials with adequate trial methodology and adequate duration of follow-up are necessary.
simple: Further randomised clinical trials with low risk of random errors and systematic errors are necessary to assess the long-term survival benefits for various treatment options in these patients.

label: ignore
complex: Nine trials involving 117,272 individuals of age 35 years or older are included in this review.
simple: Nine trials involving 117,272 adults of age 35 years or older were included in this review.

label: rephrase
complex: The trials were conducted in Australia, Finland, India, Italy, the United Kingdom and the United States, with duration of follow-up ranging from 2.1 to 12 years.
simple: The trials were conducted in Australia, Finland, India, Italy, the United Kingdom and the United States and were of high methodological quality.

label: rephrase
complex: The doses of antioxidant vitamins were higher than the recommended daily allowance.
simple: The doses of antioxidants given in each trial were higher than the recommended daily allowances.

label: merge
complex: There was no evidence of effect of antioxidant vitamin supplementation in reducing the risk of cataract, cataract extraction, progression of cataract or in slowing the loss of visual acuity.
simple: The trials provided no evidence of effect of the antioxidant vitamins beta-carotene, vitamin E and vitamin C given alone or in combination on the incidence of cataract, its extraction or progression and on the loss of visual acuity.

label: rephrase
complex: The proportion of participants developing hypercarotenodermia (yellowing of skin) while on beta-carotene ranged from 7.4% to 15.8%.
simple: Some participants (7% to 16%) on beta-carotene developed yellowing of the skin (hypercarotenodermia).

label: rephrase
complex: Two studies met criteria for inclusion in the review and 98 children were included in the meta-analysis.
simple: Data from two small studies were available for inclusion in this review; however, both studies were in infants following hospitalisation for an acute bronchiolitis illness (98 infants in total).

label: rephrase
complex: There was no significant difference between groups in the proportion of children 'not cured' at follow-up (primary outcome measure), with a pooled odds ratio (OR) of 0.61 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.24 to 1.55).
simple: There was no difference between groups in the proportion of children 'not cured' at follow-up.

label: rephrase
complex: There is currently no evidence to support the use of ICS for treatment of subacute cough in children.
simple: Without further available evidence, recommendations for the use of inhaled corticosteroids for the treatment of subacute cough in children cannot be made.

label: rephrase
complex: Further well-designed RCTs are required to support or refute the efficacy of treatment with ICS in children with subacute cough.
simple: Further well-designed studies, including children over 12 months of age, are required to determine whether treatment with inhaled corticosteroids can safely and effectively reduce the severity of subacute cough in children.

label: rephrase
complex: We included nine RCTs with 1069 participants, describing 37 comparisons: six were NSAIDs versus placebo and three were NSAIDs versus NSAIDs.
simple: This review found nine studies (1069 participants of both genders, including children, adults and older people from the USA, Japan, Belgium and Denmark) that compared various NSAIDs either with each other or with an inactive substance that has no treatment value (placebo).

label: rephrase
complex: For outcomes related to the analgesic effects of NSAIDs (headache, ear pain, and muscle and joint pain) the treatment produced significant benefits.
simple: Our findings suggest that NSAIDs may improve most analgesia-related symptoms caused by the common cold (headache, ear pain, and muscle and joint pain), but there is no clear evidence that NSAIDs are effective in improving coughs and runny noses caused by the common cold.

label: ignore
complex: The quality of the evidence may be estimated as 'moderate' because of imprecision.
simple: The quality of the evidence may be estimated as 'moderate' because of imprecision.

label: ignore
complex: The major limitations of this review are that the results of the studies are quite diverse and the number of studies for one result is quite small.
simple: The major limitations of this review are that the results of the studies are quite diverse and the number of studies for each outcome is quite small.

label: ignore
complex: NSAIDs are somewhat effective in relieving the discomfort caused by a cold but there is no clear evidence of their effect in easing respiratory symptoms.
simple: NSAIDs are somewhat effective in relieving the discomfort caused by a cold but there is no clear evidence of their effect in easing respiratory symptoms.

label: ignore
complex: The balance of benefit and harms needs to be considered when using NSAIDs for colds.
simple: The balance of benefit and harms needs to be considered when using NSAIDs for colds.

label: split
complex: Nine randomised clinical trials with 879 participants fulfilled our inclusion criteria.
simple: We searched the medical literature for randomised clinical trials (where people are allocated at random to one of two or more treatment groups) in order to perform an analysis of the role of radiotherapy administered externally for advanced liver cancer. We found nine randomised clinical trials including a total of 879 people with advanced liver cancer. We also identified seven ongoing randomised clinical trials.

label: ignore
complex: All trials were at high risk of bias, and we rated the evidence as low to very low quality.
simple: All trials were at high risk of bias, and we rated the evidence as low to very low quality.

label: rephrase
complex: All of the included trials compared combined external beam radiotherapy plus chemoembolisation versus chemoembolisation alone in people with unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma; moreover, three of the trials compared external beam radiotherapy alone versus chemoembolisation alone.
simple: Most of the included trials compared combined radiotherapy and chemoembolisation versus chemoembolisation alone.

label: rephrase
complex: All trials were conducted in China.
simple: All of the included trials were conducted in China.

label: ignore
complex: The median age in most of the included trials was around 52 years, and most trial participants were male.
simple: The average age in most of the included studies was around 52 years, and most trial participants were male.

label: ignore
complex: The median follow-up duration ranged from one to three years.
simple: The average follow-up duration ranged from one to three years.

label: rephrase
complex: We found very low- and low-quality evidence suggesting that combined external beam radiotherapy and chemoembolisation may be associated with lower mortality and increased complete and overall response rates, despite an increased toxicity as expressed by a higher rise of bilirubin and alanine aminotransferase.
simple: When compared with chemoembolisation alone, combined radiotherapy plus chemoembolisation may be associated with fewer deaths and more tumour size reduction, despite being associated with an increased risk for non-life-threatening adverse effects such as a higher rise of bilirubin and alanine aminotransferase.

label: rephrase
complex: A high risk of systematic errors (bias) as well as imprecision and inconsistency suggest that these findings should be considered cautiously and that high-quality trials are needed to assess further the role of external beam radiotherapy for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma.
simple: The low quality of evidence suggests that these results should be considered cautiously and that high-quality randomised trials should be performed to assess further the role of external beam radiotherapy for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma.

label: rephrase
complex: Only 19 women in this study were histologically confirmed with atypical complex hyperplasia before treatment.
simple: It was conducted in Norway and included 153 women, but only 19 of the women had a confirmed histological diagnosis of atypical endometrial hyperplasia; the other women had other types of endometrial hyperplasia.

label: rephrase
complex: The evidence was of low or very low quality.
simple: The quality of the evidence was low or very low for all outcomes.

label: ignore
complex: The included study was at low risk of bias, but the quality of the evidence was very seriously limited by imprecision and indirectness.
simple: The included study was at low risk of bias, but the quality of the evidence was very seriously limited by imprecision and indirectness.

label: rephrase
complex: We did not find any RCTS comparing the LNG-IUS or oral progestogens versus placebo in women with atypical endometrial hyperplasia.
simple: The included RCT compared LNG-IUS versus oral continuous or cyclic medroxyprogesterone (MPA) for treating endometrial hyperplasia.

label: rephrase
complex: Among the 19 women with atypical complex hyperplasia, after six months of treatment there was insufficient evidence to determine whether there was a difference in regression rates between the LNG-IUS group and the progesterone group (odds ratio (OR) 2.76, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.26 to 29.73; 1 RCT subgroup, 19 women, very low-quality evidence).
simple: After six months of treatment, there was insufficient evidence to determine whether there was a difference in regression rates between the LNG-IUS group and the MPA group.

label: ignore
complex: The rate of regression was 100% in the LNG-IUS group (n = 6/6) and 77% in the progesterone group (n = 10/13).
simple: The rate of regression was 100% in the LNG-IUS group (n = 6/6) and 77% in the MPA group (n = 10/13).

label: ignore
complex: Among the total study population (N = 153), over the six months' treatment the main adverse effects were nausea and vaginal bleeding.
simple: Among the total study population (N = 153), over the six months' treatment the main adverse effects were nausea and vaginal bleeding.

label: merge
complex: There was no evidence of a difference between the groups in rates of nausea (OR 0.58, 95% CI 0.28 to 1.18; 1 RCT, 153 women, very low-quality evidence).
simple: There was no evidence of a difference between the groups in rates of nausea, but vaginal bleeding was more common in the LNG-IUS group.

label: split
complex: We retrieved 2398 citations: 30 studies were eligible for further examination of their full text, and we found one registered clinical trial in progress.
simple: We identified 2398 potential studies, 30 of which we looked at in detail. We found one registered trial that is investigating our review question, but it is still ongoing and not yet completed or published.

label: rephrase
complex: No studies could be included in the analysis or review.
simple: We did not analyse the quality of the evidence as we did not find any suitable studies to include in our review.

label: split
complex: We assigned one study as awaiting classification, as it has not been accepted for publication.
simple: We found one suitable study, however although the study is complete the manuscript has not yet been accepted for publication, and so we were unable to analyse the data. We have assigned the study as awaiting classification; once its results are published we will evaluate it again and decide if it is eligible for inclusion in the review.

label: merge
complex: This systematic review failed to locate relevant published RCTs to support or refute the assertion that vascular access specialist teams are superior to the generalist model.
simple: We failed to locate any published randomized controlled trials (RCTs) to support or refute the assertion that vascular access specialist teams are superior to the generalist model for device insertion and prevention of failure.

label: ignore
complex: However, this conclusion may change once the one study awaiting classification and one ongoing study are published.
simple: However, this conclusion may change once the one study awaiting classification and the one ongoing study are published.

label: rephrase
complex: There is a need for good-quality RCTs to evaluate the efficacy of a vascular access specialist team approach for vascular access device insertion and care for the prevention of failure.
simple: There is a need for good-quality RCTs to evaluate the efficacy of a VAST approach for VAD insertion and prevention of failure.

label: rephrase
complex: We included 22 RCTs (3703 women).
simple: Twenty-two randomised controlled trials, including a total of 3703 women, met the inclusion criteria of this review.

label: rephrase
complex: Most of these studies included women with unexplained infertility.
simple: Most women had a type of infertility known as unexplained infertility, which means that after all routine tests were done, there was no obvious explanation for why the couple had not become pregnant so far.

label: rephrase
complex: Evidence suggests that if the chance of live birth/ongoing pregnancy with no intervention/a sham procedure is assumed to be 13%, then the chance with endometrial injury would be 17% to 28%.
simple: Evidence suggests that if the chance of live birth with no intervention/a placebo procedure is assumed to be 34%, then the chance with endometrial injury would be 27% to 55%.

label: rephrase
complex: Timing of intentional endometrial injury Four trials compared endometrial injury performed in the cycle before IUI to that performed in the same cycle as IUI.
simple: Four trials compared endometrial injury performed in the cycle before IUI to such injury performed in the same cycle as IUI.

label: rephrase
complex: None of these studies reported the primary outcomes of live birth/ongoing pregnancy and pain during the procedure.
simple: Live birth/ongoing pregnancy or pain during the procedure was not reported.

label: rephrase
complex: One study compared endometrial injury in the early follicular phase (EFP; Day 2 to 4) to endometrial injury in the late follicular phase (LFP; Day 7 to 9), both in the same cycle as IUI.
simple: One trial compared endometrial injury performed early in the first half of the menstrual cycle (Day 2 to 4) to endometrial injury performed late in the first half of the menstrual cycle (Day 7 to 9), both in the same cycle as IUI.

label: split
complex: The primary outcome live birth/ongoing pregnancy was not reported, but the study did report the other primary outcome of pain during the procedure assessed by a zero-to-ten VAS.
simple: Live birth/ongoing pregnancy was not reported. This study reported pain assessed by a zero-to-ten visual scale, where 0 is pain-free and 10 is unbearable pain, and showed that the pain score on average was 0.17 points lower after endometrial injury early in the first half of the menstrual cycle compared to such injury late in the first half of the menstrual cycle.

label: rephrase
complex: The pooled results should be interpreted with caution, as the evidence was of low to very low quality due to high risk of bias present in most included studies and an overall low level of precision.
simple: The quality of the evidence was assessed as low to very low.

label: rephrase
complex: Furthermore, studies investigating the effect of timing of endometrial injury did not report the outcome live birth/ongoing pregnancy; therefore no conclusions could be drawn for this outcome.
simple: Furthermore, no conclusions could be drawn about whether timing of endometrial injury affects the probability of having a baby.

label: rephrase
complex: Further well-conducted RCTs that recruit large numbers of participants and minimise bias are required to confirm or refute these findings.
simple: This means that results must be treated cautiously, and further studies are needed to confirm findings.

label: ignore
complex: Current evidence is insufficient to support routine use of endometrial injury in women undergoing IUI or attempting to conceive via sexual intercourse.
simple: Current evidence is insufficient to support routine use of endometrial injury in women undergoing IUI or attempting to conceive via sexual intercourse.

label: rephrase
complex: For this update, we included 25 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) involving over 18,000 women and their babies.
simple: This updated review now includes 25 randomised controlled trials, involving over 18,000 women and their babies.

label: rephrase
complex: The evidence suggests that zinc supplementation may result in little or no difference in reducing preterm births (risk ratio (RR) 0.87, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.74 to 1.03; 21 studies, 9851 participants; low-certainty evidence).
simple: We found that zinc supplementation in pregnancy may make little to no difference in reducing the risk of preterm births, stillbirths, or deaths around the time of birth, when compared to no zinc supplementation or placebo.

label: rephrase
complex: Further, zinc supplementation may make little or no difference in reducing the risk of stillbirth (RR 1.22, 95% CI 0.80 to 1.88; 7 studies, 3295 participants; low-certainty evidence), or perinatal deaths (RR 1.10, 95% CI 0.81 to 1.51; 2 studies, 2489 participants; low-certainty evidence).
simple: Zinc supplementation may make little or no difference to the birthweight of babies, and probably makes little or no difference to the number of babies born either with a low birthweight or small for their gestational age, when compared with no zinc supplementation, or with giving a placebo.

label: rephrase
complex: It is unclear whether zinc supplementation reduces neonatal death, because the certainty of the evidence is very low.
simple: We cannot be sure whether zinc supplementation reduces death in newborns, because the certainty of the evidence is very low.

label: rephrase
complex: There is not enough evidence that zinc supplementation during pregnancy results in improvements in maternal or neonatal outcomes.
simple: There is not enough evidence that zinc supplementation during pregnancy results in better outcomes for women and their babies.

label: split
complex: Future research to address ways of improving the overall nutritional status of pregnant women, particularly in low-income regions, and not looking at zinc in isolation, should be an urgent priority.
simple: Finding ways to improve women's overall nutritional status, particularly in low-income areas, will do more to improve the health of mothers and babies than supplementing pregnant women with zinc alone. This should be an urgent research priority for the future.

label: rephrase
complex: We found no evidence of a difference between calcium and phosphorus supplementation versus no supplementation for neonatal growth outcomes (weight, length, head circumference) at any time point reported (two, four or six weeks postnatal age).
simple: We found no difference in growth between infants who had been given human milk supplemented with extra calcium and phosphorus and infants who had received no supplementation.

label: rephrase
complex: We identified one small trial including only 40 infants that compared supplementation of human milk with calcium and phosphorus versus no supplementation in hospitalised preterm infants.
simple: We searched for evidence in April 2016 and identified one randomised controlled trial including 40 babies.

label: rephrase
complex: We judged the evidence to be of low quality and found no evidence of differences between groups for clinically important outcomes including growth and fractures.
simple: One small trial provided the evidence, and we judged the evidence to be of low quality.

label: rephrase
complex: We conclude that evidence is insufficient to determine whether benefit or harm ensues when human milk is supplemented with calcium and/or phosphorus for the hospitalised preterm infant.
simple: Evidence is insufficient to allow a judgement as to whether extra calcium and/or phosphorus provided to preterm babies confers benefit for their bones and growth.

label: rephrase
complex: We see no advantage of conducting further trials of this intervention because with the advent of multi-component human milk fortifier, supplementation of human milk with calcium and/or phosphorus alone is no longer common practice.
simple: It is no longer very common to give calcium and phosphorus supplements alone, as human milk fortifiers now available include many other components as well as minerals to support the growth and development of preterm babies.

label: rephrase
complex: Future trials should consider assessing effects of multi-component fortifiers with different mineral compositions on clinically important outcomes during the neonatal period and in later childhood.
simple: We therefore suggest that future trials conducted to examine effects of mineral supplements in preterm babies include them in multi-component human milk fortifiers and assess clinically important outcomes into childhood.

label: rephrase
complex: Eight studies met our inclusion criteria: three RCTs and five ITS studies involving a total of 135 general practices/primary care clinics, seven hospitals and one outpatient clinic.
simple: We found eight eligible studies (three randomised controlled trials and five interrupted time series studies) involving 135 primary care clinics, seven hospitals and one outpatient clinic.

label: rephrase
complex: The studies were heterogeneous in terms of types of interventions, elective procedures and clinical conditions; this made meta-analysis unfeasible.
simple: Different interventions, elective procedures and clinical conditions across included studies made pooling of data unfeasible.

label: rephrase
complex: One ITS study evaluating prioritisation of demand through a system for streamlining elective surgery services reduced the number of semi-urgent participants waiting longer than the recommended time (< 90 days) by 28 participants/mo, while no effects were found for urgent (< 30 days) versus non-urgent participants (< 365 days).
simple: The single study that evaluated an intervention aimed at prioritising demand showed that introducing a system for streamlining elective surgery reduced the number of semi-urgent patients waiting longer than recommended, but did not affect urgent or non-urgent groups.

label: rephrase
complex: Interventions aimed at restructuring the intake assessment/referral process were evaluated in seven studies.
simple: Seven studies evaluated interventions aimed at restructuring the intake assessment/referral process.

label: rephrase
complex: Four studies (two RCTs and two ITSs) evaluated open access, or direct booking/referral: One RCT, which showed that open access to laparoscopic sterilisation reduced waiting times, had very high attrition (87%); the other RCT showed that open access to investigative services reduced waiting times (30%) for participants with lower urinary tract syndrome (LUTS) but had no effect on waiting times for participants with microscopic haematuria.
simple: Three of four studies evaluating effects of open access or direct booking/referral showed beneficial effects: One study showed reduced waiting times for open access to sterilisation through keyhole surgery; another showed that open access to investigative services may lead to reduced waiting times for patients with urinary symptoms (but not for patients with microscopic blood in urine); and one study reported that same-day scheduling reduced waiting times for those seeking child health outpatient services.

label: rephrase
complex: In one ITS study, same-day scheduling for paediatric health clinic appointments reduced waiting times (direct reduction of 25.2 days, and thereafter a decrease of 3.03 days per month), while another ITS study showed no effect of a direct booking system on proportions of participants receiving a colposcopy appointment within the recommended time.
simple: One study showed no effect of a direct booking system on the proportion of patients reported to have moderate or severe cell changes on the neck of the womb who received an appointment for further investigation within four weeks.

label: split
complex: One RCT and one ITS showed no effect of distant consultancy (instant photography for dermatological conditions and telemedicine for ear nose throat (ENT) conditions) on waiting times; another ITS study showed no effect of a pooled waiting list on the number of participants waiting for uncomplicated spinal surgery.
simple: Two studies of distant consultancy (instant photography for skin conditions and telemedicine for ear, nose and throat conditions) showed no effect on waiting times to see a specialist. One study reported that using a pooled waiting list did not change the number of patients waiting for routine back surgery within the recommended time.

label: rephrase
complex: We found no studies evaluating interventions to increase capacity or to ration demand.
simple: We found no studies evaluating interventions aimed at increasing capacity or rationing demand.

label: ignore
complex: As only a handful of low-quality studies are presently available, we cannot draw any firm conclusions about the effectiveness of the evaluated interventions in reducing waiting times.
simple: As only a handful of low-quality studies are presently available, we cannot draw any firm conclusions about the effectiveness of the evaluated interventions in reducing waiting times.

label: ignore
complex: However, interventions involving the provision of more accessible services (open access or direct booking/referral) show some promise.
simple: However, interventions involving the provision of more accessible services (open access or direct booking/referral) show some promise.

label: split
complex: Three trials (20,412 women) met our inclusion criteria.
simple: We searched for evidence on 28 May 2017 and found three trials which met the inclusion criteria for the review. We identified three trials (involving 20,412 women).

label: split
complex: Two trials (20,212 women) compared intravenous (IV) TXA with placebo or standard care and were conducted in acute hospital settings (labour ward, emergency department) (in high-, middle- and low-income countries).
simple: However, one of the trials (based in Iran) did not report important outcomes, therefore, our findings are based on two trials (involving 20,212 women) conducted in hospital settings in high-, middle- and low-income countries. One was a large trial that included more than 20,000 women, and both studies looked at the effectiveness and safety of intravenous (IV) TXA compared with placebo (dummy treatment) or no treatment.

label: rephrase
complex: The larger of these (The WOMAN trial) contributed over 99% of the data and was assessed as being at low risk of bias.
simple: The trial contributing most of the information to the review was at low risk of bias.

label: rephrase
complex: The data show that IV TXA reduces the risk of maternal death due to bleeding (risk ratio (RR) 0.81, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.65 to 1.00; two trials, 20,172 women; quality of evidence: moderate).
simple: Our results show that TXA reduces the risk of maternal death due to bleeding (quality of evidence: moderate).

label: rephrase
complex: There were fewer deaths from all causes in women receiving TXA, although the 95% CI for the effect estimate crosses the line of no effect (RR 0.88, 95% CI 0.74 to 1.05; two trials, 20,172 women, quality of evidence: moderate).
simple: There were fewer deaths from all causes but the findings were uncertain (quality of evidence: moderate).

label: rephrase
complex: Results from one trial with 151 women suggest that blood loss of ≥ 500 mL after randomisation may be reduced (RR 0.50, 95% CI 0.27 to 0.93; one trial, 151 women; quality of evidence: low).
simple: In one trial with a small sample size additional blood loss of 500 mL or more was also reduced (151 women; quality of evidence: low).

label: split
complex: TXA did not reduce the risk of serious maternal morbidity (RR 0.99, 95% CI 0.83 to 1.19; one trial, 20,015 women; quality of evidence: high), hysterectomy to control bleeding (RR 0.95, 95% CI 0.81 to 1.12; one trial, 20,017 women; quality of evidence: high) receipt of blood transfusion (any) (RR 1.00, 95% CI 0.97 to 1.03; two trials, 20,167 women; quality of evidence: moderate) or maternal vascular occlusive events (any), although results were imprecise for this latter outcome (RR 0.88, 95% CI 0.54 to 1.43; one trial, 20,018 women; quality of evidence: moderate).
simple: TXA had little or no effect on the risk of serious maternal illness (quality of evidence: high), or complications such as stroke or deep venous thrombosis (quality of evidence: moderate). Rates of hysterectomy to control bleeding (quality of evidence: high) and blood transfusion (quality of evidence: moderate) were similar for women receiving TXA versus placebo.

label: rephrase
complex: There was an increase in the use of brace sutures in the TXA group (RR 1.19, 95% CI 1.01, 1.41) and a reduction in the need for laparotomy for bleeding (RR 0.64, 95% CI 0.49, 0.85).
simple: There was an increase in one surgical intervention (brace sutures) in the TXA group and a reduction in another (laparotomy to control bleeding) but there were no clear differences between groups for other surgical and invasive procedures.

label: rephrase
complex: TXA when administered intravenously reduces mortality due to bleeding in women with primary PPH, irrespective of mode of birth, and without increasing the risk of thromboembolic events.
simple: TXA when administered intravenously was effective in reducing mortality due to bleeding when given within three hours in women with primary postpartum haemorrhage without increasing the risk of other complications.

complex: There is moderate quality evidence that involving consumers in the development of patient information material results in material that is more relevant, readable and understandable to patients, without affecting their anxiety.
simple: There is moderate quality evidence from two trials that involving consumers in the development of patient information material results in material that is more relevant, readable and understandable, without affecting anxiety.

label: ignore
complex: This 'consumer-informed' material can also improve patients' knowledge.
simple: This 'consumer-informed' material can also improve knowledge.

label: rephrase
complex: There is low quality evidence that using consumer interviewers instead of staff interviewers in satisfaction surveys can have a small influence on the survey results.
simple: Two trials, which compared using consumer interviewers with staff interviewers as data collectors for patient satisfaction surveys, found small differences in satisfaction survey results, with less favourable results obtained when consumers were the interviewers.

label: rephrase
complex: There is low quality evidence that an informed consent document developed with consumer input (potential trial participants) may have little if any impact on understanding compared to a consent document developed by trial investigators only.
simple: One trial comparing two informed consent documents, one developed with consumer input and the other developed by the trial investigators, showed that consumer input may have little if any impact on understanding of the trial described in the consent document.

label: rephrase
complex: There is very low quality evidence that telephone discussions and face-to-face group meetings engage consumers better than mailed surveys in order to set priorities for community health goals.
simple: One trial, comparing two different methods for involving the public (telephone discussion and a face-to-face group meeting), showed that a face-to-face meeting is most likely to engage consumers and may result in different community health priorities.

label: merge
complex: There is little evidence from randomised controlled trials of the effects of consumer involvement in healthcare decisions at the population level.
simple: This review shows that little research has been done to find the best ways of involving consumers in healthcare decisions at the population level.

label: rephrase
complex: We included 13 studies: one cluster randomised controlled trial (CRCT) and 12 ITS studies, involving 40 hospitals, 51 intensive care units (ICUs), 27 wards, and more than 3504 patients and 1406 healthcare professionals.
simple: We identified 13 studies: one cluster randomised controlled trial (CRCT) and 12 interrupted time series (ITS) studies, involving 40 hospitals, 51 intensive care units (ICUs), 27 wards and more than 1406 healthcare professionals and 3504 patients, which assessed the impact of different interventions to reduce the occurrence of device-related infections for inclusion in this review.

label: rephrase
complex: We judged all included studies to be at moderate or high risk of bias.
simple: We judged all studies to be at moderate to high risk of bias.

label: rephrase
complex: Six of the included studies reported post-intervention adherence scores ranging from 14% to 98%.
simple: The results of six studies that reported adherence/non-adherence with infection control recommendations showed very varying adherence scores ranging from 14% to 98%.

label: rephrase
complex: The effect on rates of infection were mixed and the effect sizes were small, with the largest median effect for the change in level (interquartile range (IQR)) for the six CLABSI studies being observed at three months follow-up was a decrease of 0.6 (-2.74 to 0.28) cases per 1000 central line days (six studies and 36 sites).
simple: The effect sizes were small with the largest median effect for studies addressing central line associated blood stream infections (CLABSIs) occurring immediately after the implementation of an intervention to improve adherence to guidelines, in the majority of studies this change was not sustained over longer follow-up times.

label: rephrase
complex: This change was not sustained over longer follow-up times.
simple: The median effect for studies aiming to reduce ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) was somewhat greater and was sustained up to 12 months follow-up.

label: ignore
complex: The low to very low quality of the evidence of studies included in this review provides insufficient evidence to determine with certainty which interventions are most effective in changing professional behaviour and in what contexts.
simple: The low to very low quality of the evidence of the studies included in this review provides insufficient evidence to determine with certainty which interventions are most effective in changing professional behaviour and in what contexts.

label: ignore
complex: However, interventions that may be worth further study are educational interventions involving more than one active element and that are repeatedly administered over time, and interventions employing specialised personnel, who are focused on an aspect of care that is supported by evidence e.g. dentists/dental auxiliaries performing oral care for VAP prevention.
simple: However, interventions that may be worth further study are educational interventions consisting of more than one active element and that are repeatedly administered over time, and interventions employing dedicated personnel, who are focused on a certain aspect of care that is supported by evidence e.g. dentists/dental auxiliaries providing oral care.

label: rephrase
complex: In this review update, we added one study with 219 children.
simple: This review update (up to 26 August 2016) includes two clinical trials that compared antibiotics with placebo for children in the post-acute bronchiolitis phase (> 14 days).

label: rephrase
complex: A total of two RCTs with 249 children (n = 240 completed) were eligible for inclusion in this review.
simple: This review update includes a total of two trials with 249 children (n = 240 completed).

label: rephrase
complex: Both studies contributed to our primary and secondary outcomes, but we assessed the quality of evidence for our three primary outcomes as low, owing to the small numbers of studies and participants; and high attrition in one of the studies.
simple: Both studies contributed to primary and secondary outcomes, but the quality of evidence was low.

label: rephrase
complex: Data showed no significant differences between treatment groups for our secondary outcome: proportion of children (n = 240) with wheeze at six months (OR 0.47, 95% CI 0.06 to 3.95; random-effects model).
simple: Review authors noted no significant differences between treatment groups for our primary outcomes: proportion of children (n = 249) who had persistent symptoms at follow-up, and number of children (n = 240) rehospitalised with respiratory illness within six months; nor for our secondary outcome: proportion of children (n = 240) with wheeze at six months.

label: rephrase
complex: One study reported bacterial resistance, but only at 48 hours (thus with limited applicability for this review).
simple: One study reported bacterial resistance, but only at 48 hours.

label: ignore
complex: Another study reported adverse events from which all children recovered and remained in the study.
simple: One study reported adverse events from which all children recovered and remained in the study.

label: rephrase
complex: Current evidence is insufficient to inform whether antibiotics should be used to treat or prevent persistent respiratory symptoms in the post-acute bronchiolitis phase.
simple: Currently, not enough evidence is available to inform whether antibiotics should be used to treat or prevent persistent respiratory symptoms in the post-acute phase of bronchiolitis.

label: merge
complex: Future RCTs are needed to evaluate the efficacy of antibiotics for reducing persistent respiratory symptoms.
simple: Clinical trials are needed to evaluate the efficacy of antibiotics for reducing persistent respiratory symptoms, especially in countries where morbidity of bronchiolitis is high (e.g. indigenous populations).

label: rephrase
complex: Five trials were included in this review, representing 924 randomised children with epilepsy, all under 16 years of age at randomisation, with a median follow-up of 5.6 years.
simple: The same five trials were included in our analysis, comprising 924 epileptic children (all below 16 years old) who were randomly assigned to either early removal of AEDs (before completing two years without seizures); or late withdrawal of AEDs (after completing two years without seizures).

label: rephrase
complex: The pooled risk ratio for seizure relapse after AED withdrawal was 1.34 (95% CI 1.13 to 1.59, P = 0.0007).
simple: Considering all evidence, we found that stopping AED intake before completing two years without seizures increases the risk of seizure relapse by around 34%.

label: rephrase
complex: Overall, the included trials were classified as low or unclear risk of bias where methodological information was not reported and could not be provided by original study authors.
simple: Overall, the included trials provided a moderate quality of evidence.

label: rephrase
complex: There is evidence to support waiting for at least two seizure-free years before discontinuing AEDs in children, particularly if individuals have an abnormal EEG or partial seizures, or both.
simple: The review of trials found that there is evidence to support waiting at least two years or more seizure free before discontinuing AEDs in children, especially if they had partial seizures or abnormal EEG.

label: rephrase
complex: There is insufficient evidence to establish when to withdraw AEDs in children with generalised seizures.
simple: There is not enough evidence that demonstrates the optimal time to remove antiepileptic drugs in people (children or adults) with generalised seizures (if the electrical discharges affect the whole brain, causing global symptoms).

label: rephrase
complex: There is no evidence to guide the timing of withdrawal of AEDs in seizure-free adults.
simple: There is not enough evidence to show the best time to withdraw antiepileptic drugs in adults with epilepsy who are free of seizures.

label: rephrase
complex: Further high-quality randomised controlled trials are needed, particularly recruiting adults and recruiting those with generalised seizure types, to identify the optimal timing of AED withdrawal and risk factors predictive of relapse.
simple: More research is needed, particularly involving adults and those with generalised seizure types.

label: rephrase
complex: We included three trials involving a total of 209 participants.
simple: We included a total of three trials involving 209 participants who were treated with oral zinc pills or placebo.

label: rephrase
complex: All included studies had differences in participant selection criteria, length of follow-up and outcome measurement, precluding a meta-analysis.
simple: All three included studies had differences in their participant selection, length of follow-up and outcome measurement, which prevented a meta-analysis (combining of results).

label: rephrase
complex: The participants were all adults over 18 years with subjective tinnitus, but one study conducted in 2013 (n = 109) included only elderly patients.
simple: All patients were adults over 18 years who had subjective tinnitus.

label: merge
complex: Improvement in tinnitus severity and disability Only the study in elderly patients used a validated instrument (Tinnitus Handicap Questionnaire) for this primary outcome.
simple: All three studies investigated improvement in tinnitus as their primary outcome.

label: rephrase
complex: None of the included studies reported any significant adverse effects.
simple: Three cases of mild adverse effects were reported in different participants (e.g. mild gastric symptoms).

label: rephrase
complex: The authors of this second study reported no significant difference between the zinc and placebo groups after four months: mean difference in tinnitus loudness rating scores 0.50 (95% CI -5.08 to 6.08; very low-quality evidence).
simple: The authors reported no significant difference between the groups.

label: rephrase
complex: Two studies used unvalidated instruments to assess tinnitus severity.
simple: Two studies assessed change in the overall severity of tinnitus.

label: rephrase
complex: One (with 50 participants) reported the severity of tinnitus using a non-validated scale (0 to 7 points) and found no significant difference in subjective tinnitus scores between the zinc and placebo groups at the end of eight weeks of follow-up (mean difference (MD) -1.41, 95% CI -2.97 to 0.15; very low-quality evidence).
simple: Another study (2003) reported the severity of tinnitus using a non-validated scale (0 to 7) and found a significant difference in the subjective tinnitus scores, which favoured the zinc group.

label: rephrase
complex: A third trial (n = 50) also evaluated the improvement of tinnitus using a non-validated instrument (a 0 to 10 scale: 10 = severe and unbearable tinnitus).
simple: A third study (1991) also evaluated improvement of tinnitus using a non-validated instrument (a scale of 0 to 10) and found no significant difference between groups.

label: rephrase
complex: In this study, after eight weeks there was no difference in the proportion of patients with improvement in their tinnitus, 8.7% (2/23) treated with zinc versus 8% (2/25) of those who received a placebo (RR 1.09, 95% CI 0.17 to 7.10, very low-quality evidence).
simple: The quality of the evidence is very low.

label: ignore
complex: We found no evidence that the use of oral zinc supplementation improves symptoms in adults with tinnitus.
simple: We found no evidence that the use of oral zinc supplementation improves symptoms in adults with tinnitus.

label: rephrase
complex: We included 12 studies involving 1954 participants.
simple: In this updated review, we included a total of 12 studies (including five new studies), involving 1954 participants.

label: rephrase
complex: We identified ten studies as being of methodologically poor quality and two studies as being of medium quality.
simple: We identified ten studies as being of poor methodological quality and only two studies as being of medium methodological quality.

label: rephrase
complex: We did not perform a meta-analysis but reported the results separately.
simple: Two studies separately reported one case of diarrhoea and one case of mild nausea; two trials reported no adverse events in the treatment group; and other studies did not report any adverse events.

label: split
complex: Therefore we cannot recommend any kind of Chinese medical herbal formulation as an effective remedy for sore throat.
simple: Chinese medicinal herbs may be the treatment choice for sore throat, but we cannot recommend any particular preparation or formulation over another as we did not find any well-designed studies to provide strong evidence to conclusively support or reject the use of Chinese traditional herbal medicines in the treatment of sore throat. Enhancing the quality of research into Chinese medicinal herbs for sore throat is imperative, and stronger evidence from high quality, randomised controlled trials (RCTs) are needed.

label: split
complex: A total of 1092 participants randomly assigned to the low pressure group (509 participants) and the standard pressure group (583 participants) in 21 trials provided information for this review on one or more outcomes.
simple: A total of 1092 patients were studied in 21 trials. Patients were assigned to a low pressure group (509 patients) or a standard pressure group (583 patients).

label: rephrase
complex: Most of the trials included low anaesthetic risk participants undergoing elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
simple: Most of the trials included low surgical risk patients undergoing planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

label: rephrase
complex: One trial including 140 participants was at low risk of bias.
simple: Only one trial including 140 participants was at low risk of bias (low chance of arriving at wrong conclusions because of study design).

label: rephrase
complex: The remaining 20 trials were at high risk of bias.
simple: The remaining 20 trials were at high risk of bias (high chance of arriving at wrong conclusions because of trial design).

label: ignore
complex: The overall quality of evidence was low or very low.
simple: The overall quality of evidence was very low.

label: rephrase
complex: No mortality was reported in either the low pressure group (0/199; 0%) or the standard pressure group (0/235; 0%) in eight trials that reported mortality.
simple: No deaths were reported in either low pressure or standard pressure groups in eight trials that reported deaths (total of 434 patients in both groups).

label: rephrase
complex: One participant experienced the outcome of serious adverse events (low pressure group 1/179, 0.6%; standard pressure group 0/215, 0%; seven trials; 394 participants; RR 3.00; 95% CI 0.14 to 65.90; very low quality evidence).
simple: One participant experienced the outcome of serious adverse events (low pressure group 1/179, 0.6%; standard pressure group 0/215, 0%).

label: ignore
complex: Quality of life, return to normal activity, and return to work were not reported in any of the trials.
simple: Quality of life, return to normal activity, and return to work were not reported in any of the trials.

label: rephrase
complex: The difference between groups in the conversion to open cholecystectomy was imprecise (low pressure group 2/269, adjusted proportion 0.8%; standard pressure group 2/287, 0.7%; 10 trials; 556 participants; RR 1.18; 95% CI 0.29 to 4.72; very low quality evidence) and was compatible with an increase, a decrease, or no difference in the proportion of conversion to open cholecystectomy due to low pressure pneumoperitoneum.
simple: The difference in the percentage of people undergoing conversion to open operation (from key-hole operation) between the low pressure group (2/269; 0.8%) and the standard pressure group (2/287; 0.7%) was imprecise.

label: rephrase
complex: No difference in the length of hospital stay was reported between the groups (five trials; 415 participants; MD -0.30 days; 95% CI -0.63 to 0.02; low quality evidence).
simple: No difference was noted in the length of hospital stay between the groups.

label: rephrase
complex: Operating time was about two minutes longer in the low pressure group than in the standard pressure group (19 trials; 990 participants; MD 1.51 minutes; 95% CI 0.07 to 2.94; very low quality evidence).
simple: Operating time was about two minutes longer (very low quality evidence) in the low pressure group than in the standard pressure group.

label: none
complex: However, no evidence is currently available to support the use of low pressure pneumoperitoneum in low anaesthetic risk patients undergoing elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
simple: Currently no evidence is available to support the use of low pressure pneumoperitoneum in low surgical risk patients undergoing planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

label: ignore
complex: The safety of low pressure pneumoperitoneum has to be established.
simple: The safety of low pressure pneumoperitoneum has to be established.

label: rephrase
complex: Forty-four eligible trials, involving 8436 participants were identified.
simple: Forty-four randomised trials, involving 8436 participants were identified as suitable for this review.

label: rephrase
complex: Compared to placebo, adjuvant therapy reduces off-time, levodopa dose, and improves UPDRS scores in PD patients who develop motor complications on levodopa therapy.
simple: The review confirms reports from individual trials that, compared to placebo, add-on therapy (on a background of levodopa) significantly reduces patient off-time, reduces the required levodopa dose and improves overall disability scores (measured on the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale - UPDRS).

label: rephrase
complex: However, this is at the expense of increased dyskinesia and numerous other side-effects.
simple: However, dyskinesia and other side-effects such as constipation, hallucinations and vomiting are increased with adjuvant therapy.

label: split
complex: Indirect comparisons suggest that dopamine agonist therapy may be more effective than COMTI and MAOBI therapy, which have comparable efficacy.
simple: Indirect comparisons of the three drug classes (dopamine agonists, COMTIs and MAOBIs) suggest that dopamine agonists may provide more effective symptomatic control than COMTI and MAOBI therapy. COMTI and MAOBI have comparable efficacy.

label: rephrase
complex: However, as indirect comparisons should be interpreted with caution, direct head-to-head randomised trials assessing the impact of these different drug classes on overall patient-rated quality of life are needed.
simple: However these observations are based on indirect comparisons between trials, so could be due to other factors, e.g. differences in the types of people included in the trials, and so should to be interpreted with caution.

label: rephrase
complex: We included thirty trials, all conducted in high income countries, involving 4691 participants.
simple: We found 30 trials involving 4691 participants, examining several types of contracts.

label: rephrase
complex: Trials evaluated contracts in addiction (10 trials), hypertension (4 trials), weight control (3 trials) and a variety of other areas (13 trials).
simple: The main health problems targeted were substance addictions, hypertension and overweight.

label: split
complex: Fifteen trials reported at least one outcome that showed statistically significant differences favouring the contracts group, six trials reported at least one outcome that showed differences favouring the control group and 26 trials reported at least one outcome without differences between groups.
simple: In 15 of the trials there was at least one outcome showing statistically significant differences in favour of the contracts group (although some of the improvements in adherence did not remain when measured after a longer period). In six trials at least one outcome showed such differences in favour of the control group. In 26 trials there was at least one outcome for which there was no difference between the contract and control groups.

label: rephrase
complex: There is limited evidence that contracts can potentially contribute to improving adherence, but there is insufficient evidence from large, good quality studies to routinely recommend contracts for improving adherence to treatment or preventive health regimens.
simple: There is not enough reliable evidence available to recommend the routine use of contracts in health services to improve patients' adherence to healthcare activities or other outcomes.

label: none
complex: We were unable to find any differences in efficacy when comparing classes of antidepressants.
simple: This review compared the efficacy, withdrawal rates and side effects of different antidepressant classes in the treatment of depression in older people.

label: rephrase
complex: Our findings suggest that SSRIs and TCAs are of the same efficacy.
simple: Our main findings indicate that tricyclic antidepressants (classical and tricyclic related) and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are equally efficacious.

label: split
complex: However, we have found some evidence suggesting that TCA related antidepressants and classical TCAs have different side effect profiles and are associated with differing withdrawal rates when compared with SSRIs.
simple: However, when comparing the two tricyclic groups with SSRIs we found that tricyclic related antidepressants were similar to SSRIs in terms of overall withdrawal rate, and classical tricyclic antidepressants were associated with a higher withdrawal rate due to side effects. These findings are reflected in the differing side effect profiles when comparing both tricyclic groups with SSRIs.

label: rephrase
complex: The review suggests that classical TCAs are associated with a higher withdrawal rate due to side effect experience, although these results must be interpreted with caution due to the heterogeneity of the drugs and patient populations.
simple: The findings of the review must be interpreted with some caution in view of the relatively low patient numbers and lack of side effect data.

label: rephrase
complex: We included six RCTs with a total of 248 participants; none of the trials were determined to have overall low risk of bias.
simple: We searched the databases to August 2013, we identified six randomized trials in cardiac and elective surgical settings that compared fibrinogen concentrate (248 participants) with placebo/other sources or no treatment.

label: rephrase
complex: We found 12 ongoing trials, from which we were unable to retrieve any data.
simple: Additionally, we found 12 ongoing trials, but we were unable to retrieve any data from them.

label: rephrase
complex: Our analyses on blood transfusion data suggest a beneficial effect of fibrinogen concentrate in reducing the incidence of allogenic transfusions (RR 0.47, 95% CI 0.31 to 0.72) but show no effect on other predefined outcomes, including adverse events such as thrombotic episodes.
simple: We could not identify beneficial effects of fibrinogen concentrate on patient survival.

label: rephrase
complex: Currently, weak evidence supports the use of fibrinogen concentrate in bleeding patients, as tested here in primarily elective cardiac surgery.
simple: However, all trials were of low quality and were small, so evidence in support of fibrinogen concentrate in patients with bleeding remains weak.

label: merge
complex: We included eight RCTs with 311 participants in this review.
simple: Eight trials including 311 participants met the inclusion criteria for the review, of whom 306 participants were included in various comparisons.

label: rephrase
complex: Five trials (240 participants) investigated the three main treatments: open necrosectomy (121 participants), minimally invasive step-up approach (80 participants), and peritoneal lavage (39 participants) and were included in the network meta-analysis.
simple: The treatments compared in five trials included necrosectomy, peritoneal lavage, and the step-up approach.

label: rephrase
complex: Three trials (66 participants) investigated the variations in the main treatments: early open necrosectomy (25 participants), delayed open necrosectomy (11 participants), video-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach (12 participants), endoscopic minimally invasive step-up approach (10 participants), minimally invasive step-up approach (planned surgery) (four participants), and minimally invasive step-up approach (continued percutaneous drainage) (four participants).
simple: Three other trials compared variations in timing of necrosectomy and methods of step-up approach.

label: rephrase
complex: The trials included infected or sterile necrotising pancreatitis of varied aetiology.
simple: The participants in the trials had infected or sterile pancreatic necrosis resulting from varying causes.

label: rephrase
complex: Overall, short-term mortality was 30% and serious adverse events rate was 139 serious adverse events per 100 participants.
simple: Overall, the short-term death rate (mortality over a short time) was 30% and serious adverse events (side effects or complications) rate was 139 per 100 participants.

label: ignore
complex: The differences in short-term mortality and proportion of people with serious adverse events were imprecise in all the comparisons.
simple: The differences in short-term mortality and percentage of people with serious adverse events were imprecise in all the comparisons.

label: rephrase
complex: The number of serious adverse events and adverse events were fewer in the minimally invasive step-up approach compared to open necrosectomy (serious adverse events: rate ratio 0.41, 95% CI 0.25 to 0.68; 88 participants; 1 study; adverse events: rate ratio 0.41, 95% CI 0.25 to 0.68; 88 participants; 1 study).
simple: The number of serious adverse events and adverse events were fewer in the minimally invasive step-up approach compared to open necrosectomy.

label: rephrase
complex: The proportion of people with organ failure and the mean costs were lower in the minimally invasive step-up approach compared to open necrosectomy (organ failure: OR 0.20, 95% CI 0.07 to 0.60; 88 participants; 1 study; mean difference in costs: USD -11,922; P value < 0.05; 88 participants; 1 studies).
simple: The percentage of people with organ failure and the average costs were lower in the minimally invasive step-up approach compared to open necrosectomy.

label: rephrase
complex: There were more adverse events with video-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach group compared to endoscopic-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach group (rate ratio 11.70, 95% CI 1.52 to 89.87; 22 participants; 1 study), but the number of interventions per participant was less with video-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach group compared to endoscopic minimally invasive step-up approach group (difference in medians: 2 procedures; P value < 0.05; 20 participants; 1 study).
simple: The number of adverse events were more with the video-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach compared to the endoscopic-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach but the total numbers of procedures performed were less with the video-assisted minimally invasive step-up approach compared to the endoscopic minimally invasive step-up approach.

label: ignore
complex: The differences in any of the other comparisons for number of serious adverse events, proportion of people with organ failure, number of adverse events, length of hospital stay, and intensive therapy unit stay were either imprecise or were not consistent.
simple: The differences in any of the other comparisons for number of serious adverse events, percentage of people with organ failure, number of adverse events, length of hospital stay, and intensive therapy unit stay were either imprecise or were not consistent.

label: rephrase
complex: None of the trials reported long-term mortality, infected pancreatic necrosis (trials that included participants with sterile necrosis), health-related quality of life at any time frame, proportion of people with adverse events, requirement for additional invasive intervention, time to return to normal activity, and time to return to work.
simple: None of the trials reported long-term mortality, infected pancreatic necrosis (in trials that included participants with sterile necrosis), health-related quality of life (which measures physical, mental, emotional, and social functioning), percentage of people with adverse events, requirement for additional invasive intervention, time to return to normal activity, and time to return to work.

label: rephrase
complex: Implications for further research on this topic will be determined after the results of this RCT are available.
simple: As a result, further studies are required on this topic.

label: rephrase
complex: This study randomised 4110 adult participants with a functioning kidney transplant and elevated homocysteine levels to folic acid plus high dose B multivitamins or low dose multivitamins who were followed for a mean 4.0 years.
simple: A single study was identified that randomised 4110 adult participants with a functioning kidney transplant to homocysteine lowering with folic acid and high dose multivitamins or to low dose multivitamins and followed them for an average of four years.

label: rephrase
complex: Despite effectively lowering homocysteine levels) in homocysteine levels at follow-up (MD -4.40 μmol/L, 95% CI -5.98 to -2.82) there was no evidence the intervention impacted on any of the outcomes reported including cardiovascular mortality (RR 0.91, 95% CI 0.69 to 1.20), all-cause mortality (RR 1.04, 95% CI 0.88 to 1.22), myocardial infarction (RR 1.02, 95% CI 0.77 to 1.35), stroke (RR 1.08, 95% CI 0.69 to 1.71), commencement of renal replacement therapy (RR 1.12, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.37) or all reported adverse events (RR 1.02, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.20).
simple: Despite effectively lowering homocysteine levels, there was no evidence of benefit for any of a range of cardiovascular events.

label: rephrase
complex: There is no current evidence to support the use of homocysteine lowering therapy for cardiovascular disease prevention in kidney transplant recipients.
simple: The aim of this review was to determine if homocysteine lowering therapies effectively reduce cardiovascular event rates in kidney transplant recipients.

label: split
complex: This systematic review attempted to summarise evidence from randomised clinical trials on the treatment of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
simple: This review aimed to evaluate the beneficial and harmful effects of different types and modes of antibiotic therapy in the treatment of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in cirrhotic patients. Further randomised clinical trials with an adequate design, including a large number of participants and sufficient duration should be carefully planned to provide a more precise estimate of the beneficial and harmful effects of antibiotic treatment for spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.

label: ignore
complex: Thirteen studies were included; each one of them compared different antibiotics in their experimental and control groups.
simple: Thirteen trials were included; each one of them compared different antibiotics in their experimental and control groups.

label: ignore
complex: No meta-analyses could be performed, though data on the main outcomes were collected and analysed separately for each included trial.
simple: No meta-analyses could be performed, though data on the main outcomes were collected and analysed separately for each included trial.

label: split
complex: This review provides no clear evidence for the treatment of cirrhotic patients with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
simple: Based on the identified evidence, we cannot suggest the most appropriate management to treat spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in regard to the type, dosage, duration, or administration route of the antibiotic therapy. This review found no evidence that the effect or safety of one antibiotic is more beneficial than another.

label: none
complex: The unit of analysis was the individual reviewer in seven studies (722 reviewers in total), and the reviewed manuscript in three studies (491 manuscripts in total).
simple: We found 10 studies that involved a total of 1213 units of analysis (722 reviewers and 491 manuscripts).

label: rephrase
complex: In eight RCTs, participants were journal peer reviewers.
simple: Eight studies included only journal peer reviewers.

label: rephrase
complex: In two studies, the participants were grant peer reviewers.
simple: The remaining two studies included grant peer reviewers.

label: rephrase
complex: We found moderate-certainty evidence that emails reminding peer reviewers to check items of reporting checklists, compared with standard journal practice, have little or no effect on the completeness of reporting, measured as the proportion of items (from 0.00 to 1.00) that were adequately reported (mean difference (MD) 0.02, 95% confidence interval (CI) −0.02 to 0.06; 2 RCTs, 421 manuscripts).
simple: Emails reminding peer reviewers to check items of reporting checklists, compared with standard journal practice, probably have little or no effect on the completeness of reporting (evidence from 2 studies with 421 manuscripts).

label: rephrase
complex: There was low-certainty evidence that reviewer training, compared with standard journal practice, slightly improves peer reviewer ability to detect errors (MD 0.55, 95% CI 0.20 to 0.90; 1 RCT, 418 reviewers).
simple: Reviewer training, compared with standard journal practice, may slightly improve peer reviewer ability to detect errors (evidence from 1 study with 418 reviewers).

label: rephrase
complex: We found low-certainty evidence that reviewer training, compared with standard journal practice, has little or no effect on stakeholders' assessment of review quality in journal peer review (standardized mean difference (SMD) 0.13 standard deviations (SDs), 95% CI −0.07 to 0.33; 1 RCT, 418 reviewers), or change in stakeholders' assessment of review quality in journal peer review (SMD −0.15 SDs, 95% CI −0.39 to 0.10; 5 RCTs, 258 reviewers).
simple: Reviewer training, compared with standard journal practice, may have little or no effect on stakeholders' assessment of review quality (evidence from 6 studies with 616 reviewers and 60 manuscripts).

label: rephrase
complex: We found very low-certainty evidence that a video course, compared with no video course, has little or no effect on inter-reviewer agreement in grant peer review (MD 0.14 points, 95% CI −0.07 to 0.35; 1 RCT, 75 reviewers).
simple: We are unsure about the effect of a video course, compared with no video course, on agreement between reviewers (evidence from 1 study with 75 reviewers).

label: rephrase
complex: There was low-certainty evidence that structured individual feedback on scoring, compared with general information on scoring, has little or no effect on the change in inter-reviewer agreement in grant peer review (MD 0.18 points, 95% CI −0.14 to 0.50; 1 RCT, 41 reviewers, low-certainty evidence).

simple: This review included 61 trials (12,192 participants).

complex: Corticosteroids versus placebo or usual care Compared to placebo or usual care, corticosteroids probably slightly reduce 28-day mortality (risk ratio (RR) 0.91, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.84 to 0.99; 11,233 participants; 50 studies; moderate-certainty evidence).

complex: Corticosteroids reduced length of intensive care unit (ICU) stay for all participants (mean difference (MD) -1.07 days, 95% CI -1.95 to -0.19; 7612 participants; 21 studies; high-certainty evidence) and resulted in a large reduction in length of hospital stay for all participants (MD -1.63 days, 95% CI -2.93 to -0.33; 8795 participants; 22 studies; high-certainty evidence).
simple: Corticosteroids result in a large reduction in length of stay in the intensive care unit (ICU) and in hospital.

complex: Corticosteroids increase the risk of muscle weakness (RR 1.21, 95% CI 1.01 to 1.44; 6145 participants; 6 studies; high-certainty evidence).

simple: They probably do not increase the risk of superinfection.

complex: Corticosteroids increase the risk of hypernatraemia (high-certainty evidence) and probably increase the risk of hyperglycaemia (moderate-certainty evidence).
simple: They probably increase the risk of hyperglycaemia.

label: split

label: split

This area of research may also benefit from studies that investigate why certain partnerships between consumers and healthcare providers may be more successful than others, and an accompanying qualitative evidence synthesis addressing this aspect is forthcoming.

complex: We are uncertain whether acupuncture, compared with no intervention, has an effect regarding adverse events considered not to be serious (RR 0.67, 95% CI 0.43 to 1.06; I² = 0%; 3 trials; 203 participants; very low-certainty evidence) or detectable hepatitis B e-antigen (HBeAg) (RR 0.64, 95% CI 0.11 to 3.68; I² = 98%; 2 trials; 158 participants; very low-certainty evidence).

complex: We are uncertain whether acupuncture has an effect regarding the remaining separately reported adverse events considered not to be serious.

We are uncertain whether acupuncture compared with no intervention has an effect on the remaining separately reported adverse events considered not to be serious.

label: rephrase
complex: Three of the eight included trials received academic funding from government or hospital.

complex: None of the remaining five trials reported information on funding.

label: rephrase
complex: The clinical effects of acupuncture for chronic hepatitis B remain unknown.

label: split
complex: Based on available data from only one or two small trials on adverse events considered not to be serious and on the surrogate outcomes HBeAg and HBV DNA, the certainty of evidence is very low.
simple: The certainty of evidence on the use of acupuncture in people with chronic hepatitis B virus infection in terms of its beneficial or harmful effects on death, health-related quality of life, risk of dying due to HBV infection, and serious adverse events cannot be determined, as data on these outcomes were lacking. The certainty of evidence on acupuncture, when compared with no intervention, in terms of adverse events considered not to be serious, the proportion of people with detectable HBV DNA, and the proportion of people with detectable HBeAg, is very low. The very low certainty of the evidence is due to insufficient data ensuing from one, two, or very few trials with insufficient reporting.

simple: Four trials, involving a total of 237 participants, were included in the review.

label: rephrase
complex: Patient satisfaction and return to work during treatment were significantly better in the brace group.

complex: One trial compared Ace Wrap elastic bandage versus short arm plaster cast versus long arm plaster cast.

complex: The large loss to follow-up in this trial makes any data analysis tentative.

label: ignore
complex: The other trial, which compared immediate mobilisation versus short arm plaster cast versus long arm plaster cast for minimally displaced fractures, found no significant differences in outcome between these three interventions.
simple: The third trial, which compared immediate mobilisation versus short arm plaster cast versus long arm plaster cast for minimally displaced fractures, found no clear differences in outcome between these three interventions.

complex: The fourth trial, which compared two types of plates for surgical fixation, found no significant differences in functional or anatomical outcomes nor complications between the two groups.
