label: rephrase
complex: Liver histology was not significantly affected by simvastatin.
simple: The small pilot trial (n = 16 patients) assessing simvastatin versus placebo in NASH patients did not show significant effects on liver enzyme activities or liver histology.

label: rephrase
complex: The trial compared atorvastatin 20 mg daily (n = 63) versus fenofibrate 200 mg daily (n = 62) versus a group treated with a combination of the two interventions (n = 61).
simple: The other trial compared atorvastatin versus fenofibrate versus a group receiving both interventions in 186 patients with NAFLD.

label: rephrase
complex: There were no statistically significant differences between any of the three intervention groups regarding the week 54 mean activity levels of aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase, and alkaline phosphatase.
simple: There were no statistically significant differences between any of the three intervention groups regarding the 54 week mean activities of aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase, or alkaline phosphatases (liver enzymes) in the blood.

label: ignore
complex: The triglyceride levels seemed higher in the fenofibrate group compared with the atorvastatin group.
simple: The triglyceride levels seemed higher in the fenofibrate group compared with the atorvastatin group.

label: ignore
complex: Liver histology was not assessed in this trial.
simple: Liver histology was not assessed in this trial.

label: rephrase
complex: The presence of biochemical and ultrasonographic evidence of NAFLD seemed to be higher in the fenofibrate group compared with the atorvastatin group (58% versus 33%).
simple: The presence of biochemical and ultrasonographic evidence of NAFLD seemed higher in the fenofibrate group compared with the other two intervention groups.

label: ignore
complex: Three patients discontinued treatment due to myalgia and elevated serum creatine kinase activity; one from the atorvastatin group and two from the combination group.
simple: Three patients discontinued treatment due to myalgia and elevated serum creatine kinase activity, one from the atorvastatin group and two from the combination group.

label: ignore
complex: Another patient from the atorvastatin group discontinued treatment due to alanine aminotransferase activity that was over three times the upper normal limit.
simple: Another patient from the atorvastatin group discontinued treatment due to raised alanine aminotransferase activity, over three times the upper normal limit.

label: rephrase
complex: We have therefore included 35 studies, randomising 925,171 participants.
simple: We now include 35 studies covering 925,171 participants in this review.

label: rephrase
complex: Seven studies including 11,204 individuals compared adherence rates of those in an intensification of a patient care intervention (e.g. electronic reminders, pharmacist-led interventions, healthcare professional education of patients) versus usual care over the short term (six months or less), and were pooled in a meta-analysis.
simple: These interventions included electronic reminders, pharmacist-led interventions, and healthcare professional education to help people better remember to take their medications.

label: rephrase
complex: A separate analysis also showed improvements in long-term adherence rates (more than six months) using intensification of care (OR 2.87, 95% CI 1.91 to 4.29; 3 studies; 663 participants; high-quality evidence).
simple: These types of interventions when compared to standard care demonstrated significantly better adherence rates both over the short term (up to and including six months) as well as the long term (longer than six months).

label: rephrase
complex: Analyses of the effect on total cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol levels also showed a positive effect of intensified interventions over both short- and long-term follow-up.
simple: Additionally, cholesterol levels were better over both long- and short-term periods in those offered the intervention, compared to those receiving usual care.

label: rephrase
complex: Over the long term (more than six months) total cholesterol decreased by a mean of 17.57 mg/dL (95% CI 14.95 to 20.19; 2 studies; 127 participants; high-quality evidence).
simple: For the outcome total cholesterol levels at less than six months follow-up, we downgraded the evidence to low quality.

label: rephrase
complex: There was unclear risk of bias relating to blinding for most studies.
simple: However, analysis of other forms of bias indicated that generally the studies were at low risk of bias.

label: rephrase
complex: The evidence in our review demonstrates that intensification of patient care interventions improves short- and long-term medication adherence, as well as total cholesterol and LDL-cholesterol levels.
simple: We assessed the evidence for the outcomes using the GRADE system, and rated it as high quality for long-term adherence (more than six months) and for reduction in total cholesterol, and moderate quality for short-term medication adherence (up to six months) and for LDL-cholesterol levels.

label: none
complex: The intervention group (2058 women) received infection screening and treatment for bacterial vaginosis, trichomonas vaginalis and candidiasis; the control group (2097 women) also received screening, but the results of the screening program were not revealed and women received routine antenatal care.
simple: The study reported on 4155 women randomly assigned either to an intervention group (2058 women received infection screening and treatment for bacterial vaginosis, trichomonas vaginalis and candidiasis) or a control group (2097 women received screening, but the results of the screening program were not revealed).

label: rephrase
complex: The incidence of preterm birth for infants with a weight equal to or below 2500 g (low birthweight) and infants with a weight equal to or below 1500 g (very low birthweight) were significantly lower in the intervention group than in the control group (RR 0.48, 95% CI 0.34 to 0.66 and RR 0.34; 95% CI 0.15 to 0.75, respectively; both graded as moderate quality evidence).
simple: The number of low birthweight preterm infants and very low birthweight preterm infants were significantly lower in the intervention group than in the control group.

label: rephrase
complex: There is evidence from one trial that infection screening and treatment programs for pregnant women before 20 weeks' gestation reduce preterm birth and preterm low birthweight.
simple: The present systematic review found that a simple infection screening and treatment program during routine antenatal care may reduce preterm births and preterm low (below 2500 g) and very low (below 1500 g) birthweight.

label: split
complex: Infection screening and treatment programs are associated with cost savings when used for the prevention of preterm birth.
simple: The simple infection screening reduced preterm births from 5% of women in the control group to 3% in the intervention group. Moreover, an infection screening and treatment program during routine antenatal care is likely to save over EUR 60,000 for each preterm birth averted.

label: rephrase
complex: We included a total of 17 RCTs (1679 participants) comparing chest physiotherapy with no intervention or comparing different types of physiotherapy.
simple: We included 17 trials with a total of 1679 infants.

label: rephrase
complex: Five trials (246 participants) assessed percussion and vibration techniques plus postural drainage (conventional chest physiotherapy), and 12 trials (1433 participants) assessed different passive flow-oriented expiratory techniques, of which three trials (628 participants) assessed forced expiratory techniques, and nine trials (805 participants) assessed slow expiratory techniques.
simple: Five trials (246 infants) tested vibration and percussion techniques (conventional chest physiotherapy); three trials (628 infants) tested forced expiratory techniques; and nine trials (805 infants) tested slow expiratory techniques.

label: rephrase
complex: In the slow expiratory subgroup, two trials (78 participants) compared the technique with instrumental physiotherapy techniques, and two recent trials (116 participants) combined slow expiratory techniques with rhinopharyngeal retrograde technique (RRT).
simple: Two trials (80 infants) tested instrumental physiotherapy techniques, and three trials (216 infants) tested the rhinopharyngeal retrograde clearance technique (two combined with slow expiratory technique in 116 infants).

label: rephrase
complex: Clinical severity was mild in one trial, severe in four trials, moderate in six trials, and mild to moderate in five trials.
simple: Disease severity of infants was mild in one trial, severe in four trials, moderate in six trials, and a mix of mild to moderate in five trials.

label: rephrase
complex: One study did not report clinical severity.
simple: One trial did not report disease severity of infants.

label: rephrase
complex: Two trials were performed on non-hospitalised participants.
simple: Two trials were performed in ambulatory (non-hospitalised) infants, and the rest were performed in hospitalised infants.

label: rephrase
complex: The analyses showed no effects of conventional techniques on change in bronchiolitis severity status, respiratory parameters, hours with oxygen supplementation, or length of hospital stay (5 trials, 246 participants).
simple: We found no effect of conventional physiotherapy on disease severity of infants with moderate bronchiolitis.

label: merge
complex: Forced passive expiratory techniques failed to show an effect on bronchiolitis severity in time to recovery (2 trials, 509 participants; high-certainty evidence) and time to clinical stability (1 trial, 99 participants; high-certainty evidence) in infants with severe bronchiolitis.
simple: Forced expiratory techniques also failed to show an effect on bronchiolitis severity in infants with severe disease, while important adverse effects were reported.

label: rephrase
complex: Regarding slow expiratory techniques, a mild to moderate improvement was observed in bronchiolitis severity score (standardised mean difference −0.43, 95% CI −0.73 to −0.13; I2 = 55%; 7 trials, 434 participants; low-certainty evidence).
simple: Slow expiratory techniques showed a mild to moderate improvement in bronchiolitis severity, mostly in infants with moderate bronchiolitis, based on low-certainty evidence (future studies may challenge this result).

label: rephrase
complex: Also, in one trial an improvement in time to recovery was observed with the use of slow expiratory techniques.
simple: Also, one study showed an improvement in time to recovery with slow expiratory techniques in infants with moderate bronchiolitis.

label: none
complex: No effects were shown or reported for other clinical outcomes such as duration on oxygen supplementation, use of bronchodilators, or parents' impression of physiotherapy benefit.
simple: No effects were shown or reported for other clinical outcomes such as length of hospital stay, duration of oxygen supplementation, use of bronchodilators, or parents' impression of a benefit from the physiotherapy.

label: none
complex: We found high-certainty evidence that forced expiratory techniques in infants with severe bronchiolitis do not improve their health status and can lead to severe adverse effects.
simple: For some techniques, the evidence for the effect is of low certainty.

label: split
complex: Currently, the evidence regarding new physiotherapy techniques such as RRT or instrumental physiotherapy is scarce, and further trials are needed to determine their effects and potential for use in infants with moderate bronchiolitis, as well as the potential additional effect of RRT when combined with slow passive expiratory techniques.
simple: The evidence is robust for the older or more established types of physiotherapy (vibration and percussion and forced expiratory techniques) administered to hospitalised infants. The evidence is limited for slow expiratory techniques, and only anecdotal for the newest techniques (rhinopharyngeal retrograde clearance and instrumental clearance techniques), which have been explored in few trials.

label: rephrase
complex: Finally, the effectiveness of combining chest physiotherapy with hypertonic saline should also be investigated.
simple: There is little evidence on the effectiveness of chest physiotherapy in non-hospitalised infants.

label: rephrase
complex: Eight controlled trials (seven placebo controlled and one trial against an active treatment) with a total of 664 participants met the inclusion criteria.
simple: Eight studies with a total of 664 participants were included in this review.

label: ignore
complex: Three studied adverse effects of radiotherapy, three studied adverse effects of chemotherapy and two studied menopausal symptoms associated with breast cancer treatment.
simple: Three studied adverse effects of radiotherapy, three studied adverse effects of chemotherapy and two studied menopausal symptoms associated with breast cancer treatment.

complex: Two studies with low risk of bias demonstrated benefit: one with 254 participants demonstrated superiority of topical calendula over trolamine (a topical agent not containing corticosteroids) for prevention of radiotherapy-induced dermatitis, and another with 32 participants demonstrated superiority of Traumeel S (a proprietary complex homeopathic medicine) over placebo as a mouthwash for chemotherapy-induced stomatitis.
simple: Two studies with low risk of bias demonstrated benefit: one with 254 participants demonstrated benefits from calendula ointment in the prevention of radiotherapy-induced dermatitis, and another with 32 participants demonstrated benefits from Traumeel S (a complex homeopathic medicine) over placebo as a mouthwash for chemotherapy-induced stomatitis.

label: ignore
complex: Two other studies reported positive results, although the risk of bias was unclear, and four further studies reported negative results.
simple: Two other studies reported positive results, although the risk of bias was unclear, and four further studies reported negative results.

label: rephrase
complex: No serious adverse effects or interactions were reported attributable to the homeopathic medicines used.
simple: The homeopathic medicines used in all eight studies did not seem to cause any serious adverse effects or interact with conventional treatment.

label: ignore
complex: These trials need replicating.
simple: These trials need replicating.

label: rephrase
complex: We included one RCT which was a pilot study with 19 participants that compared HFNC therapy with oxygen delivery via a head box.
simple: One study (19 participants) met our inclusion criteria.

complex: The median oxygen saturation (SpO2) was higher in the HFNC group at eight hours (100% versus 96%, P = 0.04) and at 12 hours (99% versus 96%, P = 0.04) but similar at 24 hours.
simple: Oxygen saturations (blood oxygen levels) were better at eight and 12 hours in participants receiving high-flow nasal cannula therapy than in those receiving oxygen therapy via a head box, but were similar between groups at 24 hours, although this may have been due to higher oxygen flow rates in the high-flow nasal cannula group.

label: rephrase
complex: There was no clear evidence of a difference in total duration of oxygen therapy, time to discharge or total length of stay between groups.
simple: There was no clear evidence of a difference between the two groups in the duration of oxygen therapy, length of hospitalisation and time to discharge.

label: rephrase
complex: No adverse events were reported in either group and no participants in either group required further respiratory support.
simple: No adverse events were reported in either group.

label: rephrase
complex: Five ongoing trials were identified but no data were available in May 2013.
simple: The evidence is current to May 2013.

label: rephrase
complex: There is insufficient evidence to determine the effectiveness of HFNC therapy for treating infants with bronchiolitis.
simple: There is insufficient evidence to determine the effectiveness of high-flow nasal cannula therapy for treating bronchiolitis in infants.

label: rephrase
complex: The current evidence in this review is of low quality, from one small study with uncertainty about the estimates of effect and an unclear risk of performance and detection bias.
simple: However, our evidence is based on one low-quality, small study with uncertainty about the effects and some possibility of bias arising from the study methods.

label: ignore
complex: The included study provides some indication that HFNC therapy is feasible and well tolerated.
simple: The included study provides some indication that HFNC therapy is feasible and well tolerated.

label: rephrase
complex: Further research is required to determine the role of HFNC in the management of bronchiolitis in infants.
simple: Further research is required to determine the role of high-flow nasal cannula therapy in the management of bronchiolitis in infants.

label: ignore
complex: The results of the ongoing studies identified will contribute to the evidence in future updates of this review.
simple: The results of six ongoing studies identified will contribute to the evidence in future updates of this review.

label: rephrase
complex: All were conducted in the UK, and tested short-term changes in the consultation time allocated to each patient.
simple: All studies tested short-term changes in the consultation time allocated to each patient.

label: rephrase
complex: Overall, our confidence in the results was very low; most studies had a high risk of bias, particularly due to non-random allocation of participants and the absence of data on participants' characteristics and small sample sizes.
simple: Our confidence in the results of these studies is very low.

label: split
complex: We are uncertain whether altering appointment length increases primary care consultation length, number of referrals and investigations, prescriptions, or patient satisfaction based on very low-certainty evidence.
simple: Consequently, we are not certain whether changing appointment slots leads to an actual increase of the length of the consultation, number of referrals and investigations requested by the doctor, and number of medications prescribed. Likewise, it is unclear whether patients are more satisfied with the health care they receive when appointments are longer.

label: rephrase
complex: None of the studies reported on the effects of altering the length of consultation on resources used.
simple: None of the studies reported on the resources associated with lengthening appointments.

label: rephrase
complex: We did not find sufficient evidence to support or refute a policy of altering the lengths of primary care physicians' consultations.
simple: There is currently not enough evidence to say whether altering the amount of time that doctors consult with patients provides benefits or not.

label: rephrase
complex: We did not identify any randomised controlled trials that evaluated the effectiveness of sleep positioning systems on hip migration.
simple: No randomised controlled trials were found that evaluated the effectiveness of sleep positioning systems to reduce or prevent hip migration.

label: rephrase
complex: We did find two randomised cross-over trials that met the inclusion criteria in respect of secondary objectives relating to sleep quality and pain.
simple: These were cross-over trials (see Cochrane Glossary).

label: rephrase
complex: Neither study reported any important difference between sleeping in sleep positioning systems and not for sleep patterns or sleep quality (two studies, 21 children, very low quality evidence) and pain (one study, 11 children, very low quality evidence).
simple: Neither study reported any differences in quality of sleep or pain whether using or not using their sleep positioning system.

label: rephrase
complex: These were small studies with established users of sleep positioning systems and were judged to have high risk of bias.
simple: The children in these studies spent a few nights using their sleep positioning system and then a few nights not using it, or the other way round.

label: rephrase
complex: We found no randomised trials that evaluated the effectiveness of sleep positioning systems to reduce or prevent hip migration in children with cerebral palsy.
simple: The quality of the current evidence regarding the effectiveness of sleep positioning systems for children with cerebral palsy is very low and more robust research is needed to help families and professionals make informed decisions about whether to use this intervention.

label: ignore
complex: None of the studies reported on the outcome of death or survival with neurosensory disability.
simple: None of the studies reported on the outcome of death or survival with disability.

label: rephrase
complex: Only one trial compared surfactant delivery via thin catheter with continuation of CPAP, and one trial compared different strategies of surfactant delivery via thin catheter, precluding meta-analysis.
simple: These trials involved delivery of surfactant via a thin catheter.

label: rephrase
complex: Administration of surfactant via thin catheter compared with administration via an ETT is associated with reduced risk of death or BPD, less intubation in the first 72 hours, and reduced incidence of major complications and in-hospital mortality.
simple: Surfactant delivery via a thin catheter to spontaneously breathing preterm infants compared with surfactant administration through an ETT was associated with a decrease in the following: risk of death or BPD, need for assisted breathing in the first 72 hours of life, severe brain bleeding, death during first hospitalisation, and BPD among survivors.

label: rephrase
complex: This procedure had a similar rate of adverse effects as surfactant administration through an ETT.
simple: The procedure had rates of adverse effects similar to surfactant administration through an ETT.

label: rephrase
complex: Data suggest that treatment with surfactant via thin catheter may be preferable to surfactant therapy by ETT.
simple: These data suggest that treatment with surfactant via a thin catheter is preferable to surfactant therapy through an ETT.

label: rephrase
complex: Further well-designed studies of adequate size and power, as well as ongoing studies, will help confirm and refine these findings, clarify whether surfactant therapy via thin tracheal catheter provides benefits over continuation of non-invasive respiratory support without surfactant, address uncertainties within important subgroups, and clarify the role of sedation.
simple: Further well-designed studies of adequate size and power, as well as ongoing studies, are required to confirm and refine these findings, and to clarify whether surfactant therapy via a thin catheter provides benefits over continuation of non-invasive respiratory support without surfactant.

label: rephrase
complex: The age range of participants was from 17 to 55 years.
simple: There were 15 participants in one study and 16 in the other, their ages ranging from 17 to 55 years.

label: rephrase
complex: Both trials investigated the effectiveness of low-level laser treatment compared to placebo laser therapy on inferior alveolar sensory deficit as a result of iatrogenic injury.
simple: Low-level laser therapy was the only treatment to be evaluated in the included studies and this was compared to fake or placebo laser therapy.

label: rephrase
complex: Following treatment with laser therapy, there was some evidence of an improvement in the subjective assessment of neurosensory deficit in the lip and chin areas compared to placebo, though the estimates were imprecise: a difference in mean change in neurosensory deficit of the chin of 8.40 cm (95% confidence interval (CI) 3.67 to 13.13) and a difference in mean change in neurosensory deficit of the lip of 21.79 cm (95% CI 5.29 to 38.29).
simple: There was some evidence of an improvement when participants reported whether or not sensation was better in the lip and chin areas with low-level laser therapy.

label: rephrase
complex: No studies reported on the effects of the intervention on the remaining primary outcomes of pain, difficulty eating or speaking or taste.
simple: No studies reported on the effects of the treatment on other outcomes such as pain, difficulty eating or speaking or taste.

label: rephrase
complex: No studies reported on quality of life or adverse events.
simple: No studies reported on quality of life or harm.

label: rephrase
complex: The overall quality of the evidence was very low as a result of limitations in the conduct and reporting of the studies, indirectness of the evidence and the imprecision of the results.
simple: The overall quality of the evidence is very low as a result of limitations in the conduct and reporting of the two included studies and the low number of participants, and evidence from participants with only partial sensory loss.

label: rephrase
complex: Only four studies met the criteria for inclusion, with a total of 274 participants.
simple: Results: We identified only four trials for inclusion.

label: rephrase
complex: Four pharmacological agents were investigated: modafinil (51 participants); (−)-OSU6162, a monoamine stabiliser (12 participants of which six had a TBI); atomoxetine (60 participants); and rivastigmine (157 participants).
simple: These investigated four different drugs—modafinil; the experimental drug (−)-OSU6162; atomoxetine; and rivastigmine—against placebo.

label: split
complex: For (−)-OSU6162 modest superiority over placebo was demonstrated on three measures, but markedly inferior performance on another.
simple: The experimental drug called (−)-OSU6162 was better than placebo on three cognitive measures, although this was a small study with only six participants with TBI. In the study of (−)-OSU6162, one participant of three given placebo experienced adverse effects requiring a dose reduction, with no drop-outs reported.

label: rephrase
complex: Rivastigmine was better than placebo on one primary measure, and a single cognitive outcome in a secondary analysis of a subgroup with more severe memory impairment at baseline.
simple: Modafinil, atomoxetine and rivastigmine were not found to be better than placebo.

label: rephrase
complex: The study of modafinil assessed clinical global improvement (n = 51, low quality evidence), and did not find any difference between treatment and placebo.
simple: No difference between modafinil and placebo was found on assessment of clinical global improvement.

label: rephrase
complex: There were no other differences in safety between treatment and placebo.
simple: On most measures there was no difference between treatment and placebo.

label: ignore
complex: No studies reported any deaths.
simple: No studies reported any deaths.

label: rephrase
complex: There is insufficient evidence to determine whether pharmacological treatment is effective in chronic cognitive impairment in TBI.
simple: Conclusion: Recommendations for, or against, drug treatment of chronic cognitive impairment in TBI cannot be made on the basis of current evidence.

label: rephrase
complex: For modafinil and atomoxetine no positive effects were found.
simple: More people taking modafinil, atomoxetine and rivastigmine experienced adverse effects than those on placebo, although the difference is most likely due to chance.

label: rephrase
complex: We included one trial with a total of 60 children with a diagnosis of ASD who randomly received hyperbaric oxygen therapy or a sham treatment.
simple: We found a single, small study of 60 children that evaluated high-pressure oxygen therapy for ASD.

label: rephrase
complex: Using GRADE criteria, we rated the quality of the evidence as low because of the small sample size and wide confidence intervals (CIs).
simple: The quality of the evidence is low.

label: rephrase
complex: Overall, study authors reported no improvement in social interaction and communication, behavioral problems, communication and linguistic abilities, or cognitive function.
simple: There was no evidence that high-pressure oxygen therapy improved social interaction, behavioral problems, speech or language communication, or mental function in children with ASD.

label: rephrase
complex: With regard to the safety of hyperbaric oxygen therapy (adverse events), they reported minor-grade ear barotrauma events.
simple: However, children who received high-pressure (hyperbaric) oxygen therapy showed an increased occurrence of ear barotrauma events when compared with those in the control group.

label: rephrase
complex: To date, there is no evidence that hyperbaric oxygen therapy improves core symptoms and associated symptoms of ASD.
simple: Evidence is insufficient to confirm that high-pressure oxygen is an effective treatment for individuals with ASD.

label: split
complex: We identified six RCTs (707 women), eligible for inclusion in this updated review, however, three RCTs had mixed populations (that is, they included pregnant women with gestational diabetes) and did not report data separately for the relevant subset of women for this review.
simple: We searched for evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on 31 October 2016 and included six RCTs (707 women). Three RCTs included women with current gestational diabetes and did not report data separately for the population of women relevant to this review.

label: rephrase
complex: Therefore we have only included outcome data from three RCTs; data were available for 241 women and their infants.
simple: Therefore we have only included outcome data from three RCTs, involving 241 pregnant women and their infants.

label: merge
complex: The three RCTs all compared an oral anti-diabetic agent (metformin) with insulin.

simple: There was no clear difference in the development of pre-eclampsia (high blood pressure and protein in the urine) for women who received metformin compared with insulin (2 RCTs; 227 women; very low-quality evidence), though women receiving metformin were less likely to have pregnancy-induced high blood pressure in one RCT (206 women; low-quality evidence).

label: rephrase
complex: Women receiving metformin were less likely to have a caesarean section compared with those receiving insulin (RR 0.73, 95% CI 0.61 to 0.88; RCTs = 3; participants = 241; low-quality evidence).

label: rephrase

complex: Dornase alfa probably reduced the number of pulmonary exacerbations in trials of up to two years (moderate-quality evidence).

simple: One trial (43 children) showed a greater improvement in lung function with dornase alfa compared to hypertonic saline and one trial (23 participants) reported no difference in lung function between dornase alfa and mannitol or dornase alfa and dornase alfa plus mannitol.

simple: Mobility and balance also improved with a physiotherapy intervention, with significant improvements reported in one test of mobility (the Timed Up & Go test, which times how long it takes a person to get up from a chair, walk a certain distance, then walk back to the chair and sit down) and in two tests of balance (one assessing how far a person can reach before he or she loses balance (Functional Reach Test) and another assessing multiple aspects of balance (Berg Balance Scale)).

In the capitation arm, the dentists tended to see their patients less frequently and tended to carry out fewer fillings and extractions, but tended to give more preventive advice.

simple: According to moderate quality evidence, when we pooled the results of the six trials with the necessary information there was no significant difference in change in disability grade after four weeks. complex: In this review NTZ was found to be well tolerated over a follow-up period of two years: the number of patients experiencing at least one AE (including severe and serious AEs) during this period did not differ between NTZ-treated patients and controls.

label: rephrase

simple: The included studies provided no clear evidence about which treatment - thrombolysis or surgery - was a better option for preventing limb amputation (limb salvage) and no clear evidence about which treatment was better for preventing death or improving amputation rates within one month, six months, or one year after initial treatment.

label: rephrase
complex: Participants treated by initial thrombolysis had a greater reduction in the level of intervention required, compared with a pre-intervention prediction, at 30 days (OR 9.06, 95% CI 4.95 to 16.56, 2 studies, 502 participants).
simple: Although people receiving initial thrombolysis had increased risk of some complications, they showed greater reduction in the level of intervention required compared with that predicted before intervention.

label: rephrase
complex: None of the included studies evaluated time to thrombolysis as an outcome.
simple: None of the studies included in this review used methods to stop participants or researchers or outcome assessors from knowing what treatment they were assigned to.

label: rephrase
complex: There is currently no evidence in favour of either initial thrombolysis or initial surgery as the preferred option in terms of limb salvage, amputation, or death at 30 days, six months, or one year.

complex: Low-quality evidence suggests that thrombolysis may be associated with higher risk of haemorrhagic complications and ongoing limb ischaemia (distal embolisation).
simple: Those receiving thrombolysis treatment may be at higher risk of complications such as bleeding.

complex: The higher risk of complications must be balanced against risks of surgery in each individual case.
simple: The higher risks of complications with thrombolysis have to be weighted against individual risks in surgery.

complex: We used GRADE criteria to assess the quality of the evidence as generally low.
simple: The quality of the evidence was generally low.

complex: 'Risk of bias' assessment indicated that trial design and conduct were generally adequate; the most common areas of weakness were in blinding and allocation concealment.

simple: Probiotics make little to no difference to the risk of needing acaesarean section(6 studies, 1520 women; high-quality evidence), and probably make little to no difference to weight gain during pregnancy (4 studies, 853 women; moderate-quality evidence) or to the risk of giving birth to abig baby(4 studies, 919 women; moderate-quality evidence).

simple: We identified 67 randomised clinical trials involving a total of 6197 participants that met our inclusion criteria.

complex: All the trials were at high risk of bias.

label: rephrase
complex: A total of 5771 participants from 64 trials provided data for one or more outcomes included in this review.

label: ignore
complex: There was no evidence of differences in most of the comparisons, and where there was, these differences were in single trials, mostly of small sample size.

label: rephrase
complex: We summarise only the evidence that was available in more than one trial below.
simple: So, we mention only the evidence that was available in more than one trial.

label: rephrase
complex: Among the secondary outcomes, the only differences we found from more than one trial under the pair-wise comparison were the following: blood transfusion (proportion) was higher in the low central venous pressure group than in the acute normovolemic haemodilution plus low central venous pressure group (OR 3.19, 95% CrI 1.56 to 6.95; 208 participants; 2 studies; low-quality evidence); blood transfusion quantity (red blood cells) was lower in the fibrin sealant group than in the control (MD −0.53 units, 95% CrI −1.00 to −0.07; 122 participants; 2; very low-quality evidence); blood transfusion quantity (fresh frozen plasma) was higher in the oxidised cellulose group than in the fibrin sealant group (MD 0.53 units, 95% CrI 0.36 to 0.71; 80 participants; 2 studies; very low-quality evidence); blood loss (MD −0.34 L, 95% CrI −0.46 to −0.22; 237 participants; 4 studies; very low-quality evidence), total hospital stay (MD −2.42 days, 95% CrI −3.91 to −0.94; 197 participants; 3 studies; very low-quality evidence), and operating time (MD −15.32 minutes, 95% CrI −29.03 to −1.69; 192 participants; 4 studies; very low-quality evidence) were lower with low central venous pressure than with control.
simple: Among the secondary outcomes, the only evidence of difference was in the following: Blood transfusion (percentage): higher in the low central venous pressure group than in the acute normovolemic haemodilution (diluting the blood by giving fluids during operation) plus low central venous pressure group.

label: ignore
complex: For the other comparisons, the evidence for difference was either based on single small trials or there was no evidence of differences.
simple: For other comparisons, the evidence for difference was based on single small trials, or there was no evidence of differences.

label: ignore
complex: None of the trials reported health-related quality of life or time needed to return to work.
simple: None of the trials reported health-related quality of life or time needed to return to work.

label: rephrase
complex: Low-quality evidence also suggests that the proportion of people requiring a blood transfusion is higher with low central venous pressure than with acute normovolemic haemodilution plus low central venous pressure; very low-quality evidence suggests that blood transfusion quantity (red blood cells) was lower with fibrin sealant than control; blood transfusion quantity (fresh frozen plasma) was higher with oxidised cellulose than with fibrin sealant; and blood loss, total hospital stay, and operating time were lower with low central venous pressure than with control.
simple: Blood loss, total hospital stay, and operating time: lower with the low central venous pressure group than control.

label: rephrase
complex: There is no evidence to suggest that using special equipment for liver resection is of any benefit in decreasing the mortality, morbidity, or blood transfusion requirements (very low-quality evidence).
simple: There is no evidence to suggest that using special equipment for liver resection is of any benefit.

simple: There were substantial differences between the studies, including the training protocol, duration of training sessions (10 to 30 minutes) and duration of the intervention (over 3 to 25 weeks).

label: split

simple: Given the insufficient evidence found in this review, we believe that there is a need for more well conducted studies in order to assess the efficacy of IMT in people with asthma, including children.

label: rephrase

simple: We included 32 trials (2281 participants), published up to November 2019.

complex: Single trials compared shock wave therapy to ultrasound-guided glucocorticoid needling, ultrasound-guided hyaluronic acid injection, transcutaneous electric nerve stimulation (TENS), no treatment or exercise; dual session shock wave therapy to single session therapy; and different delivery methods of shock wave therapy.
simple: Single trials compared shock wave therapy to other treatments including ultrasound-guided glucocorticoid needling, transcutaneous electric nerve stimulation (TENS), exercise, or no treatment; or different regimens of shock wave therapy.

complex: However, in one trial (74 participants), at 3 months follow up, 14/34 participants reported pain relief of 50% or greater with shock wave therapy compared with 15/40 with placebo (risk ratio (RR) 1.10, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.62 to 1.94); low-quality evidence (downgraded for bias and imprecision).
simple: 42 out of 100 people reported pain relief of 50% or greater with shock wave therapy compared with 38 out of 100 with placebo.

simple: Pain (higher scores mean more pain) (nine trials): • Improved pain by 8% (ranging from 2% better to 14% better) or 0.78 points better (ranging from 0.17 better to 1.4 better) on a 0- to 10-point scale.

complex: Mean function (scale 0 to 100, higher scores indicate better function) was 66 points with placebo and 7.9 points better (1.6 better to 14 better, clinically important difference 10 points) with shock wave therapy (9 trials, 612 participants), moderate-quality evidence (downgraded for bias).
simple: People who had shock wave therapy rated their function as 74 points and people who had placebo rated their function as 66 points.

complex: Participant-reported success was reported by 58/150 people in shock wave therapy group compared with 35/137 people in placebo group (RR 1.59, 95% CI 0.87 to 2.91; 6 trials, 287 participants), low-quality evidence (downgraded for bias and imprecision).
simple: Participant-reported success (six trials): • 15% (ranging from 3% fewer to 49% more) more people reported their treatment a success.

complex: Withdrawal rate or adverse event rates may not differ between extracorporeal shock wave therapy and placebo, but we are uncertain due to the small number of events.
simple: We are uncertain if withdrawals or side effects differed between groups due to small number of events.

complex: Subgroup analyses indicated that there were no between-group differences in pain and function outcomes in participants who did or did not have calcific deposits in the rotator cuff.
simple: It did not appear to matter if participants had calcific deposits or not.

complex: Based upon the currently available low- to moderate-certainty evidence, there were very few clinically important benefits of shock wave therapy, and uncertainty regarding its safety.

complex: We included ten studies involving data from 944 adults, all male.
simple: We identified 10 relevant studies involving data from 944 adults, all male.

complex: Five trials involved primarily cognitive behavioural interventions (CBT) (n = 664).
simple: Five of the trials we found involved 664 men and used primarily cognitive behavioural interventions (CBT).

simple: Behavioural programmes were looked at in four trials involving 70 men.

complex: For two studies (both cross-over, n = 29) no disaggregated data were available.
simple: For two studies, not enough data were reported to assess the effectiveness of treatment.

complex: In these two studies, results for the primary outcome (being 'charged with anomalous behaviour') were encouraging, with only one new charge for the treated groups over one year in the former study, and in the latter study, only one new charge (in the drug-only group) over two years.

complex: One study compared psychodynamic intervention with probation.
simple: One study, involving 231 men, compared psychodynamic intervention with standard care, which was probation, and suggested that probation was mildly superior in terms of reducing reoffending.

complex: Data for adverse events, 'sexually anomalous urges' and for secondary outcomes thought to be 'dynamic' risk factors for reoffending, including anger and cognitive distortions, were limited.
simple: Data for adverse events, 'sexually anomalous urges' and for secondary outcomes thought to be 'dynamic' risk factors for reoffending, including anger and cognitive distortions, were limited.

label: merge
complex: The inescapable conclusion of this review is the need for further randomised controlled trials.
simple: We concluded that further randomised controlled trials are urgently needed in this area, so that society is not lured into a false sense of security in the belief that once the individual has been treated, then their risk of reoffending is reduced.

complex: Current available evidence does not support this belief.

simple: Three studies were identified that met the inclusion criteria.

complex: It was not possible to pool the data from the 3 included studies, either because of the different lengths of treatment or choice of different control treatments, or because the outcome measures were not comparable.
simple: It was not possible to pool the data from the 3 included studies, either because of the different lengths of treatment or choice of different control treatments, or because the outcome measures were not comparable.

complex: There were no statistically significant differences between validation and social contact or between validation and usual therapy.

complex: There were no assessments of carers.
simple: There were no assessments of carers.

complex: There is insufficient evidence from randomised trials to allow any conclusion about the efficacy of validation therapy for people with dementia or cognitive impairment.
simple: All in all there is insufficient evidence from randomised trials to allow any conclusion about the efficacy of validation therapy for people with dementia or cognitive impairment.

complex: The completion date of these trials is currently unknown.
simple: We believe there are currently two trials underway which may provide some evidence on this topic once completed.

complex: One trial showed the ring group had lower mean AUC insulin than the levonorgestrel-COC group (MD -204.51; 95% CI -389.64 to -19.38).

complex: With a dienogest COC, an extended-use group had greater mean change in AUC glucose (MD 82.00; 95% CI 10.72 to 153.28).
simple: In one using a dienogest pill, the extended-use group had more glucose change.

complex: In a small trial using two levonorgestrel COCs, the lower-dose group showed smaller mean change in fasting glucose (MD -3.00; 95% CI -5.89 to -0.11), but the obese and normal weight women did not differ significantly.

complex: Current evidence suggests no major differences in carbohydrate metabolism between different hormonal contraceptives in women without diabetes.
simple: In women without diabetes, hormone contraceptives have little effect on the body's carbohydrate use.

simple: Few studies compared the same types of birth control. Therefore, we cannot make strong statements.

simple: There were no cases of symptomatic DVT or PE either in the calf-thigh compression group or the plantar compression group in the first three weeks after the THR.

complex: There is a lack of evidence from randomized controlled trials to make an informed choice of IPC device for preventing venous thromboembolism (VTE) following total hip replacement.

simple: However, other outcomes such as imaging-diagnosed asymptomatic VTE were not determined and it is not possible to draw reliable conclusions from this single study with a high risk of bias.

simple: We found 23 trials (involving a total of 1339 children and adults using intermittent catheterisation for bladder emptying) comparing different catheterisation techniques and catheter designs.

label: rephrase
complex: Aseptic versus clean technique We are uncertain if there is any difference between aseptic and clean techniques in the risk of symptomatic UTI because the evidence is low-certainty and the 95% confidence interval (CI) is consistent with possible benefit and possible harm (RR 1.20 95% CI 0.54 to 2.66; one study; 36 participants).

complex: We identified no data relating to the risk of adverse events comparing aseptic and clean techniques or participant satisfaction or preference.
simple: We identified no data relating to the risk of adverse events.

label: rephrase
complex: Single-use (sterile) catheter versus multiple-use (clean) We are uncertain if there is any difference between single-use and multiple-use catheters in terms of the risk of symptomatic UTI because the certainty of evidence is low and the 95% CI is consistent with possible benefit and possible harm (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.55, 1.74; two studies; 97 participants).
simple: We are uncertain if there is any difference between single-use and multiple-use catheters in the risk of symptomatic UTI because the certainty of evidence is low.

label: rephrase
complex: One study comparing single-use catheters to multiple-use catheters reported zero adverse events in either group; no other adverse event data were reported for this comparison.

complex: We identified no data for participant satisfaction or preference.

simple: We are uncertain if there is any difference between hydrophilic and uncoated catheters in the number of people with symptomatic UTI. We are uncertain if there is any difference in patient satisfaction or preference.

complex: Uncoated catheters probably slightly reduce the risk of urethral trauma and bleeding compared to hydrophilic-coated catheters (RR 1.37, 95% CI 1.01 to 1.87; moderate-certainty evidence).
simple: Uncoated catheters probably slightly reduce the risk of urethral trauma and bleeding compared to hydrophilic-coated catheters.

label: ignore
complex: The current research evidence is uncertain and design and reporting issues are significant.
simple: The current research evidence is uncertain and design and reporting issues are significant.

complex: More well-designed trials are needed.
simple: More well-designed trials are needed.

complex: Such trials should include analysis of cost-effectiveness because there are likely to be substantial differences associated with the use of different catheterisation techniques and strategies, and catheter designs.
simple: Such trials should include analysis of cost-effectiveness because there are likely to be substantial differences associated with the use of different catheter designs, catheterisation techniques and strategies.

label: rephrase
complex: We included 28 studies, of which 26 provided data on 2407 participants (average age 24 years).
simple: We found 28 studies, of which 26 had usable data on 2407 participants with recent-onset or first-episode schizophrenia.

label: rephrase
complex: The mean sample size in the included studies was 92 participants (ranging from 19 to 444) and duration ranged between 26 and 52 weeks.
simple: The studies had a duration of between 26 and 52 weeks for the intervention phase and, in 18 studies, participants were contacted again to collect further data after the end of the treatment.

label: rephrase
complex: When looking at the results at combined time points (mainly up to one year after start of the intervention), CBT added to standard care was associated with a greater reduction in overall symptoms of schizophrenia (standardised mean difference (SMD) -0.27, 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.47 to -0.08, 20 RCTs, n = 1508, I2 = 68%, substantial heterogeneity, low certainty of the evidence), and also with a greater reduction in positive (SMD -0.22, 95% CI -0.38 to -0.06, 22 RCTs, n = 1565, I² = 52%, moderate heterogeneity), negative (SMD -0.20, 95% CI -0.30 to -0.11, 22 RCTs, n = 1651, I² = 0%) and depressive symptoms (SMD -0.13, 95% CI -0.24 to -0.01, 18 RCTs, n = 1182, I² = 0%) than control.
simple: When looking at the results about one year after the start of the intervention, we found that CBT added to standard care: - is more effective than control conditions in reducing the overall symptoms of schizophrenia - is more effective than control conditions in reducing positive symptoms of schizophrenia (e.g. hearing voices) - is more effective than control conditions in reducing negative symptoms of schizophrenia (e.g. apathy, loss of interest and motivation, lack of concentration) - is more effective than control conditions in reducing depressive symptoms of schizophrenia - is more effective than control conditions in improving the global state (number of participants with symptoms improvement, number of participants with symptoms worsening, scores on scales that measure the general level of symptoms of the participants) - is more effective than control conditions in improving functioning (e.g. participating in social life, taking part in everyday activities).

label: rephrase
complex: This review synthesised the latest evidence on CBT added to standard care for people with a first-episode or recent-onset psychosis.
simple: We wanted to find out the effects of cognitive behavioural therapy added to standard care for people with a first episode or recent onset of schizophrenia.

complex: We obtained full-text copies of nine potential studies and included five trials with a total of 244 participants in this review.
simple: This review included five randomised controlled trials involving 244 participants.

complex: Our primary outcome of a composite score was used in the two trials comparing radiotherapy versus sham radiotherapy and showed a risk ratio of success of 1.92 (95% confidence interval (CI) 1.27 to 2.91) in favour of orbital radiotherapy.
simple: A combined analysis of trials using our primary outcome showed that the 'risk' of success in a patient who received radiotherapy was 1.92 times that of a patient who did not receive radiotherapy.

label: merge
complex: In a single trial no difference between radiotherapy and steroid monotherapy was found.
simple: Single studies suggested that orbital radiotherapy in combination with steroids had a better outcome than steroids alone.

complex: No significant changes in quality-of-life scores following treatment with radiotherapy versus alternative treatments were found.

label: rephrase
complex: Three studies (212 participants) were included in this review.
simple: The review authors searched the medical literature up to March 3 2015, and identified three relevant clinical trials (212 participants) that investigated ozone therapy for the treatment of foot ulcers in people with diabetes.

label: merge
complex: One trial (101 participants) compared ozone treatment with antibiotics for foot ulcers in people with DM.
simple: One trial, with 101 participants, compared ozone treatment with antibiotics and followed up for 20 days.

label: rephrase
complex: This study showed that ozone treatment was associated with a greater reduction in ulcer area from baseline to the end of the study than treatment with antibiotics (MD -20.54 cm2, 95% CI -20.61 to -20.47), and a shorter duration of hospitalisation (MD -8.00 days, 95% CI -14.17 to -1.83), but did not appear to affect the number of ulcers healed over 20 days (RR 1.10, 95% CI 0.87 to 1.40).
simple: The results of this study showed that the reduction in ulcer size was greater, and also the length of hospital stay was shorter, in those receiving ozone treatment, but there was no apparent benefit in terms of the number of foot ulcers healed.

label: rephrase
complex: No side effects were observed in either group.
simple: No adverse effects (side effects or harms) were observed with either treatment.

label: rephrase
complex: The other two trials (111 participants) compared ozone treatment plus usual care with usual care for foot ulcers in people with DM.
simple: The other two trials (111 participants) compared ozone treatment plus usual care with usual care.

label: rephrase
complex: The meta-analysis results did not show evidence of a difference between groups for the outcomes of reduction of ulcer area (MD -2.11 cm2, 95% CI -5.29 to 1.07), the number of ulcers healed (RR 1.69, 95% CI 0.90 to 3.17), adverse events (RR 2.27, 95% CI 0.48 to 10.79), or amputation rate (RR 2.73, 95%CI 0.12, 64.42).
simple: The results of these two studies showed that there were no apparent differences between the groups for reduction in ulcer size,the number of foot ulcers healed, or occurrence of adverse events and amputation rates.

label: rephrase
complex: The available evidence was three small RCTs with unclear methodology, so we are unable to draw any firm conclusions regarding the effectiveness of ozone therapy for foot ulcers in people with DM.
simple: On the basis of the limited and poor quality information available, the review authors were unable to draw any conclusions about the effectiveness of ozone therapy for treating foot ulcers in people with DM.

complex: Asthenia, diarrhoea, insomnia, mood change, hallucinations and dehydration occurred at incidence rates of 5% and below.

complex: We found no direct evidence that opioids affected patient consciousness, appetite or thirst when used to treat cancer pain.

complex: There are now 30 studies involving 6334 participants (6339 fractures) included in this review, which was published in 2011.

We judged the overall certainty of the evidence for mouth self-examination and remote screening to be very low, and downgraded for variation across studies, applicability of the study samples, and imprecise accuracy estimates.
